tags: Informed consent/Capacity, competency for making decisions
title: Can a Minor Refuse Assent for Emergency Care?
case:
Dr. McKinney is working in the emergency department when an ambulance arrives with a frantic 12-year-old-girl, Micah, and her 8-year-old sister, Gracie. The paramedic quickly reports that the girls were home alone when Gracie found Micah sitting on the bathroom floor screaming and “covered in blood.” No one has yet been able to contact the girls’ parents. Micah is so frantic that she is unable to give Dr. McKinney any medical history.
After a rapid assessment, it is clear to Dr. McKinney that Micah is having profuse vaginal bleeding. However, he does not yet know the reason for the bleeding, and no one knows if the young girl was assaulted or suffered some injury. Alternatively, she could have a bleeding disorder of some sort. He knows that, in either case, a severe laceration or other injury could result in life-threatening bleeding, and decides that a vaginal exam is critically necessary for Micah’s care. Recognizing that the Emergency Medical Treatment and Active Labor Act (EMTALA) protects his right to treat Micah without parental consent because of her life-threatening problem, he begins to try to examine her. She screams, “Don’t you look down there, I don’t want that! Stop it!” as she kicks and yells.
Dr. McKinney normally likes to seek the assent of young patients prior to any invasive exam, and Micah has clearly refused to provide her assent. However, he retains legal authority to perform this important exam, and begins to question the best way to proceed, as the exam will be impossible to perform on an uncooperative 12-year-old.
case_rewrite:
Dr. McKinney is on duty in the emergency department when paramedics rush in with two sisters: 12-year-old Micah, visibly distraught and blood-soaked, and her younger sister, 8-year-old Gracie, who trails behind looking terrified. The paramedic briefs Dr. McKinney: the girls were alone at home when Gracie discovered Micah on the bathroom floor, screaming and “covered in blood.” Despite their efforts, no one has reached the girls’ parents. Micah is so overwhelmed with panic that she cannot answer any of Dr. McKinney’s questions about her medical history.

Dr. McKinney conducts a rapid assessment and quickly recognizes that Micah is experiencing heavy vaginal bleeding. The cause remains unknown—there is no information yet to determine whether she has suffered an assault, sustained an accidental injury, or is experiencing symptoms of a bleeding disorder. Dr. McKinney is acutely aware that, regardless of the underlying cause, severe bleeding from a laceration or other injury could quickly become life-threatening. He determines that an urgent vaginal examination is critical to identify the source and control the bleeding.

Knowing that the Emergency Medical Treatment and Active Labor Act (EMTALA) authorizes him to proceed with necessary life-saving treatment for minors in the absence of parental consent, Dr. McKinney prepares to examine Micah. As he attempts to begin the examination, Micah recoils, screaming, “Don’t you look down there, I don’t want that! Stop it!” She thrashes and kicks, her distress escalating.

Dr. McKinney has always made it a priority to seek the assent of young patients before performing invasive procedures, and Micah’s refusal is unmistakable. However, he also recognizes that her refusal is occurring under extreme panic and trauma, and that he retains both the legal authority and ethical responsibility to proceed if it is necessary for her well-being. He pauses, wrestling with how to move forward, since it is clear that it would be impossible to safely or effectively perform the required exam on a frantic, uncooperative 12-year-old.

As the urgency of Micah’s situation mounts and the clinical details remain incomplete, Dr. McKinney considers consulting a second emergency physician to independently confirm the necessity of a prompt invasive examination. To minimize both physical and psychological harm to Micah, he contemplates the option of performing the vaginal exam under anesthesia. He approaches Micah and explains, as calmly as possible, that using anesthesia could help her sleep through the exam so he can find the cause of her bleeding and treat her more comfortably. He encourages her to agree to this plan, seeking her assent to proceed.

If Micah continues to refuse, Dr. McKinney knows he still has the legal and ethical mandate to act in her best interests. With his professional judgment, he resolves to implement the safest and most compassionate course of evaluation and treatment, balancing the urgent need to protect Micah’s life, minimize harm, and respect her dignity, even if that means proceeding with sedation and anesthesia without her consent.

ethical_issues.question: How can a physician conduct necessary medical evaluation and treatment under the principle of nonmaleficence when a minor has explicitly refused examination?
keypoints: A patient’s refusal made in a state of trauma openand panic cannot be regarded as a competent informed refusal, which forms the ethical basis for the reasonableness of subsequent interventions.

Physicians must uphold the principle of beneficence while adhering to the principle of nonmaleficence, striving to minimize physical and psychological harm to the patient, and at the same time must not delay life-saving treatment.

When the clinical history is incomplete and the situation is urgent, an independent evaluation by a second emergency physician should be sought to ensure the reasonableness and necessity of any invasive examination.

Performing a vaginal examination under anesthesia can minimize the patient’s psychological and physical distress and optimize diagnostic and therapeutic outcomes; this represents a reasonable and necessary emergency management approach.

The physician should explain to the patient the need for anesthesia in order to locate the source of bleeding and provide treatment, and should seek the patient’s assent to this plan whenever possible.

If the patient refuses to assent, based on legal authorization and ethical principles applicable in emergency situations, the physician may proceed with sedation and anesthesia without consent to safeguard the patient’s life.
The physician should adhere to their professional judgment by implementing the medical evaluation and treatment that best benefits the patient, balancing legal, ethical, and clinical considerations.

ethical_issues.question: In emergency situations, is it ethically permissible for a physician to administer sedation and anesthesia to a minor who has refused assent in order to perform necessary examinations and treatment?
keypoints: In emergency situations, U.S. law permits physicians to perform necessary medical examinations and treatments on minors without parental consent to prevent life-threatening conditions or serious harm.

Under the Emergency Medical Treatment and Active Labor Act (EMTALA), emergency departments are required to provide timely medical screening and necessary treatment to all patients presenting with emergency medical conditions, without the need for parental or patient consent.

Micah’s intense reaction does not constitute a decisionally capable informed refusal; the physician should endeavor to explain the necessity of sedation and anesthesia to her and attempt to obtain her assent.

If Micah continues to refuse assent, it is both legally permissible and ethically justifiable for the physician to proceed with sedation and anesthesia without her consent, as the intervention aims to protect the patient’s life and address urgent medical needs.

title: The Problem with Hand-Offs
case:
Aidan is on the first emergency shift of her third-year surgery rotation. When her resident hands her the file for her first patient, a 60-year-old man with textbook symptoms of appendicitis, she is excited to work on the case. Aidan meets the patient, Ed, takes a thorough history, and does a physical exam that confirms the diagnosis.
She discovers in her history taking that the patient has a diagnosis of Crohn’s disease but has not had symptoms for decades and writes this in her note. She reassures Ed that appendicitis is easily treated with a surgery that gets you home the next day. She presents the case to the resident on call. Two hours later, the resident reports to the attending on-call surgeon, who is busy with a trauma case. Hours pass and personnel change. Aidan spends time with Ed, a pleasant architect with a gentle wit.
The new attending surgeon quickly reads the patient note and orders a CT scan, thinking that the patient might be having a flare-up of Crohn’s disease. The patient waits another hour for a CT scan, which reveals an inflamed appendix. A nurse notices that the patient hasn’t gone to the OR yet and is concerned, but doesn’t feel comfortable bringing this up with the resident or the surgeon. An hour later, when Ed finally gets to the OR, his appendix has ruptured, and the surgeons have to cut out several feet of his small bowel.
When Aidan reports back to the hospital the next day, she sees that Ed hasn’t left. He has developed a surgical site infection. Ed is upset about his long wait in the emergency department and about complications from what he thought was a routine surgery. He ends up staying in the hospital for an extra week to recover.
Aidan wonders whether this outcome is the result of a medical error or errors. She goes to speak about the case with Dr. Sark, who performed Ed’s surgery. He tells her “stuff happens.” He explains that hindsight is 20/20 but that these sorts of events are inevitable in a busy emergency department. When she asks him if someone should disclose or apologize to the patient, he says no.
case_rewrite:
Aidan begins her first emergency shift as a third-year medical student on the surgery rotation, eager to participate in patient care. Her resident hands her the chart of her first patient, Ed, a 60-year-old man presenting with classic symptoms of appendicitis. Aidan meets Ed, who is a gentle, witty architect, and takes a meticulous history. She learns that Ed was diagnosed with Crohn’s disease years ago, but he has had no symptoms for decades—a fact she documents carefully in her note. After a thorough physical exam, which supports her suspicion of appendicitis, Aidan reassures Ed that this is a straightforward condition, usually resolved with a simple surgery and a quick recovery.

Aidan presents Ed’s case to the resident on call. The resident in turn updates the attending surgeon, who is preoccupied with a trauma case elsewhere in the hospital. As the hours tick by, staff shift changes occur. Aidan remains attentive to Ed, who shares stories and jokes to pass the time.

Later, a new attending surgeon assumes responsibility. He quickly skims through Ed’s chart and, noting the mention of Crohn’s disease, suspects a possible Crohn’s flare rather than appendicitis. He orders a CT scan to clarify the diagnosis. Ed waits another hour for the scan, which ultimately confirms an inflamed appendix.

A nurse, noticing that Ed has not yet been taken to the operating room, grows increasingly concerned. Despite her worries, she hesitates to raise the issue with the resident or the attending, feeling uncertain about her authority to intervene.

By the time Ed is finally brought to the operating room, his appendix has ruptured. The surgical team must remove several feet of his small intestine. The next day, Aidan returns to the hospital and is surprised to find Ed still in his bed. He has developed a surgical site infection, requiring a prolonged hospital stay and leaving him frustrated about the extended wait and unexpected complications from what he thought would be a routine procedure.

Reflecting on the case, Aidan wonders if the outcome was the result of medical errors or systemic failures. She discusses her concerns with Dr. Sark, the surgeon who performed Ed’s operation. Dr. Sark dismisses her worries, saying, “Stuff happens.” He tells her that hindsight always makes things look clearer and insists that such events are unavoidable in a busy emergency department. When Aidan asks whether someone should explain to Ed what happened or apologize, Dr. Sark firmly says no.

ethical_issues.question: When a process failure is identified, does Aidan, as a medical student, have an ethical obligation to communicate her concerns to the resident and the attending physician?
keypoints: Nurses have an ethical responsibility to promptly express concerns to the resident and attending physician when they observe that a patient has not been taken to the operating room in a timely manner, in order to ensure patient safety.
The hospital has failed to effectively implement the concept of a “just culture,” resulting in frontline staff, such as nurses, lacking the authority and support to intervene in process failures.

The healthcare team should share collective responsibility for safeguarding patient safety, respecting and accepting nurses’ reasonable interventions and feedback, and promoting the development of a patient-centered culture of care.

ethical_issues.question: Do healthcare institutions have an ethical obligation to disclose to patients the reasons for surgical delays and postoperative infections and to offer an apology?
keypoints: Healthcare institutions have an ethical obligation to honestly disclose to patients and their families the specific reasons for surgical delays and postoperative infections, and to offer an apology on behalf of the institution; this communication responsibility typically rests with the operating surgeon.

Clinicians and healthcare teams bear the responsibility to proactively identify and report systemic errors, advocate for process improvements, and prevent recurrence of similar incidents to ensure patient safety.

ethical_issues.question: Do attending physicians have an ethical responsibility to communicate directly and jointly examine the patient during hand-offs in order to avoid unnecessary investigations and delays?
keypoints: Attending physicians have an ethical responsibility during hand-offs to communicate directly and jointly examine the patient to ensure accurate transmission of clinical information, thereby avoiding unnecessary tests and treatment delays.

The new attending physician, due to lack of direct communication with the predecessor and failure to personally examine the patient, overlooked the critical information in the medical record that the patient’s Crohn’s disease had been asymptomatic for decades, and ordered a CT scan solely based on the history note, resulting in unnecessary testing and delay.

Clinicians should proactively identify and improve systemic errors within clinical workflows, bearing a dual responsibility: to provide patient care and to optimize processes that safeguard patient safety.

When failures in clinical processes cause patient harm, physicians have an ethical obligation to disclose the facts honestly to the patient, offer an apology, and implement measures to prevent recurrence of similar events.

title: When the Evidence Isn't There: Seeking Informed Consent for New Procedures
case:
Mr. Roberts had been having difficulty urinating and, because he was 68, figured it was just a normal part of the aging process. Eventually, however, Mr. Roberts noticed that he was starting to have pain with urination as well, and went to his physician to get checked out. A few weeks and a biopsy later, Mr. Roberts was told by his physician that he had prostate cancer. Mr. Roberts was frightened and filled with uncertainty. His physician told him not to worry and recommended an excellent surgeon with whom Mr. Roberts could discuss his treatment options.
That’s how Mr. Roberts ended up at Dr. Klein’s urology clinic. Dr. Klein was a renowned expert in laparoscopic prostatectomy and had built a reputation based on his unusually low rate of complications. Shortly before Mr. Roberts’ visit, Dr. Klein and his colleagues had begun offering robotic-assisted laparoscopic prostatectomy to patients like Mr. Roberts. After looking carefully at Mr. Roberts’ lab and biopsy reports, Dr. Klein believed that surgery was the best option for this patient.
Dr. Klein admitted that the robotic procedure was much newer to him than the laparoscopic approach he had been using with success for so many years, but he said that he believed it would soon become the standard protocol for uncomplicated prostatectomy. Nevertheless, Dr. Klein offered Mr. Roberts the choice between robot-assisted prostatectomy or the standard laparoscopic prostatectomy. The robotic surgery, for one thing, was costlier.
Mr. Roberts found himself presented with a decision he did not feel qualified to make. He asked Dr. Klein about the risks and benefits of the two techniques. Dr. Klein was able to give Mr. Roberts an accurate description of the risks of the laparoscopic procedure, both from his personal experience and from hard evidence collected by urologists over many years. When Mr. Roberts asked Dr. Klein to do the same for the robotic procedure, Dr. Klein had to rely upon his limited experience. He was able to tell Mr. Roberts that in the past few months, all of his patients had had a good experience with the robotic procedure, and he thought he was already beginning to see quicker recovery times with those patients. Dr. Klein admitted, however, that more objective data for the robotic procedure was still somewhat sparse, although rapidly accumulating.
Somewhat confused and more than a little frightened by the whole prospect, Mr. Roberts told Dr. Klein that he wanted to have the procedure that Dr. Klein thought was best for him and that he would abide by whatever decision Dr. Klein made.
case_rewrite:
Mr. Roberts, a 68-year-old man, had been experiencing increasing difficulty with urination. Initially, he attributed these symptoms to aging, but when pain began to accompany urination, his concern grew, prompting him to see his physician. After a series of tests and a prostate biopsy, Mr. Roberts received the unsettling news that he had prostate cancer. Overwhelmed by fear and uncertainty, he listened as his physician reassured him and recommended that he consult Dr. Klein, a highly regarded urologist, to discuss possible treatment options.

Dr. Klein was well-known for his expertise in laparoscopic prostatectomy, a procedure he had performed with a notably low complication rate. Recently, however, Dr. Klein and his team had started to offer robotic-assisted laparoscopic prostatectomy to suitable patients. Upon reviewing Mr. Roberts’ laboratory and biopsy results, Dr. Klein concluded that surgery would be the most appropriate treatment for him.

During their consultation, Dr. Klein was transparent about his experience. He explained that while he was very experienced with standard laparoscopic prostatectomy, the robotic-assisted technique was relatively new to his practice. He acknowledged that although he believed robotic surgery was likely to become the standard approach for cases like Mr. Roberts’, his own hands-on experience with the equipment was still limited compared to his expertise with traditional laparoscopy. Dr. Klein further noted that the robotic procedure was more expensive and that, although his recent patients had done well, comprehensive, long-term data on outcomes were not yet available.

Mr. Roberts was uneasy about having to choose between the established laparoscopic method and the newer robotic-assisted approach. When he asked about the risks and benefits, Dr. Klein was able to provide detailed, well-supported information about the traditional laparoscopic procedure based on years of personal experience and robust clinical data. For the robotic-assisted option, however, Dr. Klein explained that he could only speak from his limited, recent experience and the still-developing evidence base. He mentioned that early results seemed promising, particularly regarding recovery times, but emphasized that objective data was still emerging.

Feeling confused and frightened, Mr. Roberts admitted to Dr. Klein that he did not feel equipped to make such a decision. He asked Dr. Klein to recommend the option he believed would be best, expressing trust in Dr. Klein’s judgment and stating that he would follow whatever course Dr. Klein advised.

ethical_issues.question: How can a physician ensure that they have acquired the necessary skills and experience before offering a new surgical procedure, and how should they fully disclose their level of experience to the patient during the informed consent process?
keypoints: Physicians must undergo systematic training and practical experience before offering new surgical procedures to ensure they possess the necessary skills and expertise.
Physicians should objectively assess their own experience and the maturity of the technique, confirming that the new procedure is appropriate for the patient and that the risks are manageable.

During the informed consent process, physicians must explicitly disclose to patients the novelty of the procedure, the limited availability of current evidence-based data, and their own level of experience with the technique.

ethical_issues.question: When a patient is unable to make a decision due to confusion and fear, how can a physician provide substantive recommendations to assist the patient in making an informed choice while still respecting patient autonomy?
keypoints: Physicians should, while respecting patient autonomy, proactively offer substantive recommendations based on professional judgment to assist patients in clarifying their options.

Physicians must set aside personal interests and objectively evaluate the risks and potential benefits of both new and established surgical procedures, integrating the patient’s specific circumstances to assess their value.

Physicians should engage in thorough communication with patients regarding the benefits, risks, and uncertainties of the surgery, supporting patients in weighing their choices to ensure decisions align with their best interests and values.

title: Negotiating Parental Requests for Medication
case:
Mr. and Mrs. Green took their 11-year-old son, to see Dr. Frank, a pediatrician. This was the first time Dr. Frank had seen the boy, who was in the sixth grade and had played piano since he was five. He loved music and showed exceptional promise. Within the preceding month, however, he had been struggling with piano lessons and practice. The boy told Dr. Frank that piano was very important to him and that he really wanted to be accepted into a special school for the performing arts. His audition for the school was to take place in six months. But he had been having trouble concentrating and therefore had been practicing less. His parents said that "until now," the boy had never exhibited any behavioral or emotional problems at home or at school. Mr. and Mrs. Green wondered whether he had attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and whether medication could help their son with his concentration, at least until after his audition.
Dr. Frank asked the Greens if their son had been evaluated by a school psychologist. They replied that he attended a small private school with no psychologist on staff. To determine whether he met the criteria for a diagnosis of ADHD, Dr. Frank asked the Greens about symptoms of inattention, hyperactivity and impulsivity. The Greens said that, in addition to trouble with piano practice, his math teacher had contacted them regarding his forgetfulness, distractibility and daydreaming in class. Their son had always done well in math, but now he was struggling to keep a C. They stressed several times that their son's piano teacher was especially concerned about his recent distractibility.
Dr. Frank asked the Greens about the boy's mood, and they admitted that he had become somewhat withdrawn. Every day he sat down at the piano after finishing his after-school snack. Mrs. Green had noticed that he played for a few minutes and then stopped, and she would find him staring off into space, still sitting at the piano, 15 or 20 minutes later. He exhibited no signs of hyperactivity. The Greens said that there had been no major changes at home and no incidents at school that they were aware of, such as fights with friends or teachers.
During most of the appointment, the boy sat quietly in a chair with his head down. When questioned by Dr. Frank about his mood and concentration, he responded mostly with "yes" and "no" answers, and a few times he said, "I've just been having trouble concentrating." Dr. Frank did not believe that the boy met the criteria for a diagnosis of ADHD and, because the changes had only occurred in the last month, Dr. Frank was not yet concerned. He recommended some dietary changes, told Mr. and Mrs. Green to monitor their son's sleeping and activity habits, and said that he would like to schedule another appointment in six weeks to see whether the dietary changes had had any effect. Mr. Green got very angry and said, "This is ridiculous! There's clearly something wrong with the kid. Why can't we just get a prescription to try for a few months?"
case_rewrite:
Mr. and Mrs. Green brought their 11-year-old son to see Dr. Frank, a pediatrician, for the first time. Their son, a sixth grader, had a deep passion for music and had played piano since the age of five, demonstrating remarkable talent. However, over the past month, he had begun to struggle with his piano lessons and practice sessions. The boy confided in Dr. Frank that music was extremely important to him and that he aspired to be accepted into a prestigious performing arts school, with an audition scheduled in six months. Recently, though, he found it increasingly difficult to concentrate, leading to less time spent practicing.

The Greens explained that, up until this point, their son had never shown any behavioral or emotional issues either at home or at school. They were concerned that he might have developed attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and wondered whether medication could help improve his focus, at least until the audition had passed.

Dr. Frank inquired whether the boy had been assessed by a school psychologist, to which the Greens replied that his small private school did not have one on staff. To evaluate the possibility of ADHD, Dr. Frank asked the Greens about symptoms of inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity. The parents mentioned that, besides his difficulties with piano, his math teacher had reached out to them, noting his increased forgetfulness, distractibility, and tendency to daydream during class. Although their son had always excelled in math, he was now struggling to maintain a C. They repeatedly emphasized that his piano teacher was particularly troubled by his newfound distractibility.

When Dr. Frank asked about his mood, the Greens admitted that their son had become somewhat withdrawn. Each afternoon, after his after-school snack, he would sit at the piano, but after playing for only a few minutes, he would stop, and Mrs. Green would often find him staring blankly into space, still seated at the piano 15 or 20 minutes later. There were no signs of hyperactivity. The Greens stated that there had been no significant changes at home and no incidents at school, such as conflicts with friends or teachers, that they were aware of.

Throughout much of the appointment, the boy sat quietly with his head lowered. When Dr. Frank questioned him about his mood and ability to concentrate, he responded mostly with short "yes" or "no" answers and occasionally said, "I've just been having trouble concentrating." Based on the information gathered, Dr. Frank did not feel the boy met the criteria for an ADHD diagnosis. Since these difficulties had only arisen in the past month, Dr. Frank was not immediately alarmed. He suggested making some dietary adjustments, advised the Greens to monitor their son's sleep and daily activities, and recommended scheduling a follow-up visit in six weeks to assess any changes.

At this, Mr. Green grew visibly frustrated. He exclaimed, "This is ridiculous! There's clearly something wrong with the kid. Why can't we just get a prescription to try for a few months?"

ethical_issues.question: How should a physician make clinical decisions when faced with parental requests for medication?
keypoints: Physicians should base their decisions on a thorough assessment, rationally weighing the potential risks of prescribing stimulants against the possible consequences of inaction.
In the absence of clear diagnostic evidence for ADHD, prescribing stimulants may cause physical and psychological harm to the child due to unnecessary medication exposure.

Premature reliance on pharmacological treatment may obscure or delay a comprehensive evaluation of the child’s actual problems and hinder the implementation of multifaceted, non-pharmacological interventions.
Excessive or inappropriate prescribing of stimulants not only poses risks to individual health but may also contribute to medication misuse and broader public health concerns at the societal level.

Physicians should adhere to the principle of nonmaleficence by engaging patiently with parents, explaining the diagnostic rationale and treatment options, thereby fostering reasonable expectations and shared decision-making.

ethical_issues.question: How can a physician effectively conduct an initial consultation under time constraints to fulfill the principle of beneficence?
keypoints: Physicians should recognize that the requests of patients and their families may carry multiple underlying meanings; through patient communication and educational guidance, they should broaden the family’s understanding of the nature of the condition and the range of possible treatments.

Within the time constraints of an initial consultation, physicians should focus on initiating care in an effective and appropriate manner, including thorough history-taking, obtaining collateral information from teachers or other third parties, engaging in private dialogue with the child, and formulating a plan for follow-up assessment and monitoring.

ethical_issues.question: How can physicians broaden parents’ understanding of the nature of the condition and the range of possible treatments when addressing parents’ narrow demands, in order to prevent potential harm?
keypoints: Physicians should be sensitive to the underlying distress signals behind parental requests, recognizing their anxiety and expectations regarding the child’s condition, and, guided by the principle of beneficence, actively investigate the problem and seek appropriate solutions.

Physicians should provide parents with a comprehensive explanation of the differential diagnosis for the symptoms, including attention deficit disorders, depression, generalized anxiety disorder, organic causes such as malnutrition or iron deficiency, as well as the potential impact of social stressors (e.g., pressure related to the piano audition) on the child’s presentation.

Physicians should avoid prescribing medication as the sole or primary intervention, in order to prevent premature pharmacological treatment from overshadowing other potential etiologies and non-pharmacological interventions, thereby ensuring a comprehensive and evidence-based approach to diagnosis and management.

Upon recognizing the parental distress, physicians have a duty to educate and guide the family, assisting them in broadening their understanding of the nature of the condition and the possibilities for multifaceted treatment, reframing the clinical encounter to alleviate the child’s actual suffering and to avoid a narrowly focused therapeutic approach.

title: Unnecessary Antibiotics
case:
Dr. Waterman had a 12-year-old general pediatrics practice in a rapidly growing suburban community. Although well liked by his patients and their families, he found it increasingly difficult to thrive as a solo practitioner under a managed care system. After careful consideration, he decided to merge his practice with a fairly new pediatrics group in the same community.
During merger negotiations with his three new partners, Dr. Waterman agreed to a compensation formula based largely on the number of patients seen. His new partners, led by the ambitious Dr. Connolly, have also made no secret of their desire to increase the "market share" of the practice and the importance of securing a strong foothold in the community before more competition “moves in.”
In the winter, a few months after the merger, the office was flooded with anxious parents and their sneezy, sniffling and coughing youngsters. Ever since a medical school buddy told him about a case of community-acquired MRSA (methicillin-resistantStaphlococcus aureus) he had encountered, Dr. Waterman has been particularly sensitive to the threat of antibiotic resistance. He has therefore adjusted his prescribing pattern over the last few years to minimize antibiotic use. Although it requires more clinic time, Dr. Waterman is careful to explain to each parent why antibiotics might not be indicated for his or her child and what can be done to manage cough and cold symptoms at home.
The clinic was characteristically overbooked one Saturday in January, and Dr. Waterman was called in to see patients even though it was his day off. Assisted by Joan, the clinic nurse, he examined one of Dr. Connolly’s regular patients, a 4-year-old boy named Colby. Dr. Waterman told Colby’s mother that he suspected his symptoms were due to a respiratory virus and would not therefore prescribe antibiotics. She listened attentively to what he said and then became somewhat agitated, arguing that Dr. Connolly always gave Colby an antibiotic “just to be safe.” In fact, as she told him, she had spoken with Dr. Connolly that morning and he had specifically told her to bring Colby in for an antibiotic prescription. “Now, after listening to what you told me, I am just confused.” As the clinic nurse slipped out of the room, Dr. Waterman tried yet again to alleviate her anxiety and explain his reasoning.
Suddenly, Dr. Connolly opened the door and burst into the examining room with Joan at his side and gave Colby and his mother a big smile. “Everything going all right?” he asked. “I have your prescription right here. Sorry about the delay.” Later on, Dr. Connolly sympathetically told Dr. Waterman that making parents you haven’t met before “happy” isn’t always easy and that he was glad he could “help out” with Colby’s case.
That evening, after everyone had left the clinic, Dr. Waterman looked through every one of the past week’s charts. He noticed that Dr. Connolly and the other two pediatricians regularly prescribed antibiotics inappropriately. He also observed that they each managed to see about 20 percent more patients than he did.
case_rewrite:
Dr. Waterman had dedicated twelve years to building his general pediatrics practice in a rapidly expanding suburb. Well respected by his young patients and their families, he nonetheless found himself struggling to keep his solo practice afloat under the mounting pressures of managed care. After much deliberation, Dr. Waterman decided to merge his practice with a relatively new pediatric group operating in the same community.

During discussions about the merger, Dr. Waterman and his three soon-to-be partners agreed on a compensation model that heavily rewarded the number of patients seen. The group, led by the enterprising Dr. Connolly, made it clear that growing the practice’s "market share" was a top priority, especially with more competitors poised to enter the area.

A few months after the merger, as winter illnesses surged, the office was inundated with worried parents and their children suffering from coughs, sniffles, and sneezes. Ever since a former classmate had described a troubling case of community-acquired MRSA, Dr. Waterman had become acutely aware of the dangers of antibiotic resistance. Over recent years, he had intentionally reduced his antibiotic prescribing, taking extra time during appointments to explain to parents why antibiotics were often unnecessary, and how to manage cold symptoms at home.

On a typically hectic Saturday in January, the clinic was overbooked, and Dr. Waterman was asked to come in, even though it was supposed to be his day off. With help from Joan, the clinic nurse, he saw one of Dr. Connolly’s regular patients—a four-year-old boy named Colby. After examining Colby, Dr. Waterman explained to his mother that her son likely had a viral respiratory infection and that antibiotics would not help. Colby’s mother listened, but soon became upset, insisting that Dr. Connolly always prescribed antibiotics for Colby “just to be safe.” She explained that she had spoken with Dr. Connolly earlier that day, and he had instructed her to bring Colby in specifically for an antibiotic prescription. “Now I don’t know what to think,” she said, clearly frustrated. As Joan quietly left the room, Dr. Waterman patiently tried once more to reassure her and clarify his reasoning.

At that moment, Dr. Connolly swept into the exam room with Joan beside him, greeting Colby and his mother with a warm smile. “Is everything going well?” he asked cheerfully. “Here’s your prescription—sorry for the wait.” Later, Dr. Connolly told Dr. Waterman, with a sympathetic tone, that making new parents feel reassured wasn't always simple, and expressed relief that he could “help out” with Colby’s case.

That evening, after the clinic had emptied, Dr. Waterman reviewed the previous week’s patient charts. He noted that Dr. Connolly and the other two pediatricians routinely prescribed antibiotics in situations where they were not indicated. He also realized that each of his colleagues managed to see about 20 percent more patients than he did.

ethical_issues.question: How can a physician maintain professional integrity when practicing within a team that has differing clinical approaches or values?
keypoints: Physicians should proactively address differences in clinical philosophy and practice within their teams through optimized communication and interpersonal negotiation.
The team should reach consensus on key clinical areas, advocating for and implementing evidence-based medicine to prevent patient confusion arising from varying treatment approaches among physicians.
Physicians need to rationally assess their compatibility with the quality of care and values upheld by the team, engaging in necessary self-reflection and professional positioning.

Physicians must recognize the cumulative and far-reaching impact of individual clinical decisions on public health (such as antibiotic resistance) and on their own professional integrity, thereby assuming corresponding ethical responsibilities.

title: Never Symptom-Free
case:
When Dr. Alverdo saw Richard Edmunds' name on the index card of appointments that the secretary handed him, he thought, "What can I possibly say or do that will make a difference?" Mr. Edmunds, a high school English teacher, was 46 and has a history seasonal allergies. Nevertheless, Mr. Edmunds was in the office, on average, every 6 weeks. His symptoms varied but were always difficult to verify or quantify—pain, discomfort, "just not feeling right."
Three months ago, Mr. Edmunds presented with complaints of headaches. He described the pain as generalized and worse in the afternoon than in the morning. The headache was not accompanied with nausea or visual changes. They never woke him from sleep and were not associated with any particular activity or food. Non-narcotic analgesics were ineffective. Based on the history and physical, which included a normal neurological exam, Dr. Alverdo concluded that the headaches were most likely caused by muscle tension and prescribed a course of anti-inflammatory medications, muscle relaxant, and physical therapy exercises. Mr. Edmunds made 3 visits to the therapist but said the exercises were not helping. He kept asking, "How do you know I don't have a tumor or an aneurysm about to blow, Doc?" Finally, Dr. Alverdo ordered a CT scan, which, he had to admit, he believed was a "shot in the dark." The scan was negative. After that report, Mr. Edmunds complained less about the headaches. Dr. Alverdo hadn't heard about headaches for a couple of months now.
Lately, Mr. Edmunds was having chest pains. Faithful about his annual physical exam, Mr. Edmunds had no previous history of exertional angina or shortness of breath. There was no known heart disease in Mr. Edmunds' immediate family. Dr. Alverdo had ordered an EKG and cardiac stress test, both of which Mr. Edmunds passed with flying colors. Then, thinking the persistent pain might be digestion-related, Dr. Alverdo ordered a barium swallow and upper GI series. Negative.
Looking at the name on the card, Dr. Alverdo thought, what's Edmunds going to want today? At the last visit, Mr. Edmunds reported that he could not sleep because of the crushing pain. The only remaining diagnostics for chest pain were highly invasive. Dr. Alverdo resolved that he would not be talked into ordering an angiogram or anything else that would put Mr. Edmunds at risk. There simply was no indication for it. He imagined how the conversation would go. Mr. Edmunds would dispute everything Dr. Alverdo said. This generally went on for 25 or 30 minutes. "Doctor," Edmunds would say. "Do you want me to be one of those cases you read about in the paper where it says, "he kept telling the doctor he was sick, but no one believed him?"
case_rewrite:
When Dr. Alverdo glanced at the day’s schedule and saw Richard Edmunds’ name among his appointments, a familiar sense of resignation crept in. “What can I possibly say or do that will make a difference?” he wondered. Richard Edmunds, a 46-year-old high school English teacher with a background of seasonal allergies, had become a regular fixture in Dr. Alverdo’s office, showing up roughly every six weeks. The complaints changed from visit to visit but shared a common thread: vague and difficult-to-verify symptoms—pain, discomfort, or simply, as Mr. Edmunds often put it, “just not feeling right.”

Three months earlier, Mr. Edmunds had come in troubled by headaches. He described them as a generalized ache, worse in the afternoons, with no nausea or vision changes. The headaches didn’t wake him from sleep, nor did they seem tied to any activity or particular food. Over-the-counter painkillers hadn’t helped. After a thorough history and a normal neurological exam, Dr. Alverdo concluded the headaches were likely due to muscle tension. He prescribed anti-inflammatory medication, a muscle relaxant, and recommended physical therapy exercises. Mr. Edmunds attended three sessions with the therapist, but reported no relief. “How do you know I don’t have a tumor or an aneurysm about to blow, Doc?” he pressed at each visit. Eventually, Dr. Alverdo ordered a CT scan, privately acknowledging it was “a shot in the dark.” The scan results came back normal. After that, the headaches seemed to fade from Mr. Edmunds’ list of complaints, and Dr. Alverdo hadn’t heard him mention them for a couple of months.

More recently, Mr. Edmunds had begun to focus on chest pains. He was conscientious about his annual physicals and had never before reported exertional chest pain or shortness of breath. He had no known family history of heart disease. Dr. Alverdo ordered an EKG and a cardiac stress test—both of which Mr. Edmunds completed without any trouble. Suspecting the pain might be gastrointestinal, Dr. Alverdo next arranged for a barium swallow and an upper GI series. The results, once again, were negative.

Now, as Dr. Alverdo prepared for today’s appointment, he couldn’t help but wonder what new concern Mr. Edmunds would present. At their last meeting, Mr. Edmunds had described sleepless nights due to “crushing” chest pain. The remaining diagnostic options for chest pain were highly invasive, and Dr. Alverdo firmly decided he would not be pressured into ordering an angiogram or other risky procedures, given the lack of any clinical indication. He could already picture how the conversation might unfold: Mr. Edmunds would challenge each of his explanations, as he often did, prolonging their discussion for half an hour or more. “Doctor,” Mr. Edmunds would inevitably say, “do you want me to be one of those cases you read about in the paper where it says, ‘he kept telling the doctor he was sick, but no one believed him?’”

ethical_issues.question: Should physicians perform high-risk and non-indicated diagnostic tests solely to reassure patients?
keypoints: The physician’s primary obligation is to safeguard the patient’s well-being and should not perform high-risk diagnostic tests without clear indications solely to reassure the patient, as the anticipated benefit of reassurance does not justify the potential risks involved.
Patient autonomy is important, but it does not obligate physicians to provide medical interventions that may be dangerous or non-beneficial.

Physicians should make diagnostic and therapeutic decisions based on clinical probabilities and principles of evidence-based medicine; in this case, the probability of a cardiac etiology is very low and does not support the use of high-risk testing.
Ordering high-risk tests without indication may exacerbate tension and maladaptive dynamics in the physician-patient relationship, creating a vicious cycle that undermines effective care.

Physicians should attend to the patient’s psychological and emotional needs by helping them understand and accept medical uncertainty, thereby preventing excessive testing driven by vulnerability and anxiety, as well as avoiding unnecessary consumption of healthcare resources.

ethical_issues.question: How should physicians balance patient autonomy and the principle of nonmaleficence when confronted with excessive patient activism?
keypoints: Physicians should not yield to patients’ demands for dangerous or non-beneficial interventions, in order to uphold the principle of nonmaleficence and safeguard patient safety and well-being.

Physicians ought to employ effective communication to help patients understand the risks of excessive testing and the inherent uncertainties in medicine, thereby facilitating acceptance of reality and interrupting the vicious cycle of anxiety driven by compulsive reassurance-seeking.
If candid communication fails to alleviate the patient’s medical anxiety, physicians should consider referral to psychiatry for more specialized support.

title: Does Patient Autonomy Outweigh Duty to Treat?
case:
Dr. Avery, a second-year emergency medicine resident, was on duty in a large urban hospital one night with 2 other residents, when a police officer escorted in a young man. The officer had found Scott Daley semi-conscious and with a cut on his head. When questioned by the officer, Mr. Daley had responded quickly, telling the officer that he had fallen. He then stood up and walked without difficulty. To be on the safe side, the officer decided that Mr. Daley should receive medical attention. Mr. Daley agreed.
Upon physical examination, Dr. Avery documented a minor laceration on Mr. Daley's forehead and some significant occipital swelling and tenderness. He appeared slightly intoxicated but was alert and aware. He understood the physician's questions and answered basic questions, such as his name, the location, and the date. However, he did not cooperate with more detailed questioning. He said that he had hit his head on the sidewalk and that it hurt. His pupils were equally round and reactive to light. There was no obvious evidence of intracranial bleeding, but Dr. Avery said she wanted to do a CT scan to be certain there was no skull fracture or intracranial bleeding.
At this suggestion, Mr. Daley became argumentative. He did not want any tests and did not want to spend any more time in the emergency room. He said he was fine. "Just give me some aspirin or something for the headache, and I'm outta here," he said. Because of the signs of head trauma and her difficulty in completing a detailed mental status exam, Dr. Avery did not want to let Mr. Daley leave the hospital before ruling out significant internal head injury. She attempted to explain the gravity of the situation, and risks of leaving without allowing her to complete diagnostic tests. The patient seemed inattentive and refused to cooperate with further questioning.
After reasoning with the patient for several minutes to no avail, Dr. Avery asked the other residents to help her convince the patient. They were equally unsuccessful, and the patient became increasingly agitated. He said that they were "ganging up on" him. He got up and started to leave. Dr. Avery could, of course, allow the patient to leave against medical advice. Given the possibility that he could have a life-threatening head injury, though, she contemplated whether to restrain him and complete the diagnostic work-up against his will.
case_rewrite:
Dr. Avery, a second-year emergency medicine resident, was working a night shift at a busy urban hospital alongside two other residents. During her shift, a police officer arrived escorting a young man named Scott Daley. The officer explained that he had discovered Mr. Daley in a semi-conscious state with a cut on his head. When questioned at the scene, Mr. Daley quickly told the officer he had simply fallen. He then stood up and walked without difficulty. Erring on the side of caution, the officer decided Mr. Daley should receive medical evaluation, to which Mr. Daley agreed.

On examination, Dr. Avery noted a minor laceration on Mr. Daley’s forehead and marked swelling and tenderness at the back of his head. He appeared somewhat intoxicated but was awake and aware of his surroundings. He answered basic questions correctly—stating his name, the location, and the date—but he was uncooperative when Dr. Avery attempted to ask more detailed questions. Mr. Daley insisted he had hit his head on the sidewalk and complained that it hurt, but offered little more. His pupils were equal, round, and reactive to light, and there were no obvious signs of intracranial bleeding. Still, Dr. Avery recommended a CT scan to rule out a skull fracture or internal bleeding.

At the mention of further testing, Mr. Daley’s attitude changed abruptly. He became argumentative and said he didn’t want any additional tests or to spend more time in the emergency department. “Just give me some aspirin or something for this headache, and I’m outta here,” he demanded. Dr. Avery, concerned by the signs of head trauma and her inability to complete a thorough mental status exam, was reluctant to let Mr. Daley leave before excluding the possibility of a serious head injury. She tried to explain the potential seriousness of his condition and the risks of leaving without diagnostic testing, but Mr. Daley was inattentive and refused to cooperate further.

After several minutes of unsuccessful reasoning, Dr. Avery enlisted the help of the other residents to persuade him. Despite their efforts, Mr. Daley only grew more agitated, accusing the team of “ganging up on” him. He stood up, intent on leaving the department. Dr. Avery now faced a dilemma: she could allow Mr. Daley to leave against medical advice, but given the risk of a potentially life-threatening head injury and her inability to fully assess his decisional capacity due to possible intoxication and incomplete mental status evaluation, she considered whether it would be necessary to restrain him and proceed with the diagnostic work-up against his wishes.

ethical_issues.question: How should decisional capacity be assessed when a patient refuses recommended medical treatment?
keypoints: The core of decisional capacity assessment lies in whether the patient can understand the medical options, deliberate thoughtfully, and clearly express a decision.

Prior to assessment, all reversible impairments of capacity—such as intoxication, hypoxia, sedation, and stress—should be identified and addressed to ensure the patient possesses rational and autonomous judgment.
During the evaluation, alternative options and their potential consequences must be explained to the patient, and the patient’s level of understanding of this information should be confirmed.

The patient’s personal goals and values should be assessed, and input from family members or individuals familiar with the patient should be considered to determine deviations in current mental status from baseline.
The required level of decisional capacity varies according to the risk and complexity of the specific medical decision; higher levels of capacity are necessary when refusing high-risk treatments.
Decisional capacity is dynamic, with the patient’s status potentially changing over time; ongoing monitoring and timely reassessment are essential.
Informed refusal is a process encompassing assessment of decisional capacity, comprehensive disclosure of risks, and thorough documentation—not merely the signing of consent or refusal forms.

If a complete evaluation of decisional capacity cannot be performed, it is reasonable to temporarily detain the patient until such assessment is completed, with meticulous documentation of the process.

ethical_issues.question: When a patient exhibits some elements of decisional capacity but also shows evidence of impaired capacity, how should the physician assess their decisional capacity?
keypoints: Physicians should first identify and address all reversible impairing factors (such as intoxication, hypoxia, sedation, stress, etc.) to ensure that the patient possesses the capacity to make rational and autonomous decisions.

When assessing decisional capacity, the evaluation should be based on the patient’s understanding of their own goals and values, discussion of alternatives and their potential consequences, and confirmation that the patient can comprehend relevant information, deliberate thoughtfully, and clearly articulate their decision.

Decisional capacity is dynamic; a patient’s capacity may fluctuate over time and vary according to specific circumstances, and different medical decisions may require different levels of capacity—these factors must be fully considered during assessment.

Family members or individuals familiar with the patient’s baseline mental status can serve as valuable sources of collateral information to help determine deviations in the patient’s current capacity relative to their usual state.

If a comprehensive assessment of decisional capacity cannot be completed due to the patient’s condition or other factors, a reasonable and prudent approach is to temporarily detain the patient until such evaluation can be finalized or the clinical status stabilizes.

ethical_issues.question: In situations where an assessment of decisional capacity cannot be completed, is it ethically justifiable to override the patient’s wishes and implement restraints to ensure medical safety?
keypoints: The patient demonstrates partial decisional capacity (e.g., being awake, alert, and able to answer basic questions) but exhibits signs of impaired capacity, making it impossible to confirm whether he fully understands the risks of leaving against medical advice.

When a complete assessment of decisional capacity cannot be performed, it is reasonable and necessary to temporarily detain the patient until a comprehensive evaluation can be completed to ensure medical safety.

Prior to implementing restraints or restricting freedom, the patient should be clearly informed that he will be temporarily detained until the decisional capacity evaluation is finalized; such transparent communication helps alleviate patient resistance and promotes cooperation.

If the patient is able and willing to answer questions appropriately and understands the risks of leaving, his autonomy should be respected by allowing discharge; otherwise, to prevent potential life-threatening harm, necessary restraints should be applied until the evaluation is complete.

Efforts should be made to actively identify and mitigate reversible factors that may impair decisional capacity (such as alcohol intoxication, pain, anxiety, etc.) to support the patient in making the most rational and autonomous medical decisions.

title: Alignment of Abolition Medicine With Reproductive Justice
case: We get locked down at 10 o’clock. It wasn’t until twelve before anybody came. And when they opened the door, there was blood everywhere. So, the gush that I felt was blood. They were looking at me like I was an alien. Nobody wanted to touch me. They were like, “Oh, can you walk?” I remember being furious, scared, angry, and crying, “No. I’m not walking down those steps. I’ve been asking you to move me for months.” A few minutes later, the men brought a stretcher and took me out. They were debating whether to call 911 or the marshals, so at this point, I’m afraid not only for my baby but for my own life. I was hemorrhaging. I was like, “You need to get me out of here. Please call 911.”
case_rewrite: At 10 p.m., the facility doors were locked as usual. It wasn’t until two hours later, at midnight, that anyone finally came to check on me. When they opened the door, blood was everywhere. The sudden rush I had felt before had been blood loss. The staff looked at me as though I were something strange, keeping their distance and hesitating to help. No one wanted to touch me. “Can you walk?” they asked, but I was furious, terrified, and in pain, tears streaming down my face. “No. I’m not walking down those steps. I’ve been begging you to move me for months,” I cried out. Only after a few minutes did some of the men arrive with a stretcher and carry me out. As they debated whether to call 911 or the marshals, panic set in—not just for my baby’s safety, but for my own life. I was hemorrhaging badly. “You need to get me out of here. Please call 911,” I pleaded.

ethical_issues.question: How can the healthcare system critically examine and dismantle the institutional practices and structural factors that perpetuate health inequities and its complicity with mass incarceration within a framework of racial justice?
keypoints: The healthcare system bears a moral, ethical, and professional responsibility to critically examine and actively dismantle institutional structures that perpetuate health inequities and racial oppression, thereby advancing racial equity and social justice.

Healthcare practitioners must be trained in the abolitionist reproductive justice framework to both provide high-quality, accessible reproductive health services and to identify and challenge the complicity and institutional practices linking the medical system with the carceral system.

The healthcare system should prioritize the review and reform of policies that subject marginalized populations—such as incarcerated individuals, people living in poverty, and persons with disabilities—to racial discrimination and surveillance, while promoting comprehensive and stigma-free reproductive health technologies that empower these populations to exercise autonomy over their reproductive and bodily rights.

The integration of reproductive justice with abolition medicine requires healthcare practitioners to reposition their roles within prison and criminal justice systems, actively leveraging their professional authority to support efforts to end mass incarceration and the criminalization of pregnancy, thereby advancing the health rights and human rights of incarcerated people.

ethical_issues.question: How can health care professionals reimagine their roles within the carceral system to support the abolition of mass incarceration and the criminalization of pregnancy, thereby safeguarding the human rights and reproductive justice of incarcerated individuals?
keypoints: Health care professionals must reimagine their roles within the prison system, leveraging their professional authority to interrogate and dismantle racially unjust policies and structural factors, support the abolition of mass incarceration and the criminalization of pregnancy, and advance systemic transformation.

Health care professionals should be trained in the abolitionist reproductive justice framework to provide high-quality, accessible, and comprehensive reproductive health services, empower incarcerated individuals and other marginalized groups, oppose stigma, violence, and coercion, and safeguard their autonomy over reproductive rights.

Health care professionals should actively align themselves with the reproductive justice movement, with particular support for Black Lives Matter and Black women, in order to address the gendered impacts of mass incarceration and the criminalization of pregnancy, and to promote social justice.

The practice of abolition medicine should focus on three main goals: abolishing the prison industrial complex, eradicating White supremacy and racism in health care, and ensuring that incarcerated pregnant people have access to comprehensive, high-quality reproductive, sexual, and maternal health services.

title: Does Pediatric Obesity Indicate Child Neglect?
case:
At 9 years old, Tiffany is morbidly obese (above the 150th weight percentile for her age, with a BMI of 35). Tiffany was referred to a pediatric obesity clinic by her pediatrician. Over the years, her weight problem had become more pronounced, leading to impaired fasting glucose and hyperlipidemia. Her pediatrician felt the significant increase in her weight over the last 3 years warranted an intensive approach to her obesity. As the pediatric obesity clinic physician took Tiffany’s history, it became clear that she lived in an environment in which physical activity was not encouraged and fast food was a staple. Tiffany’s mother bragged that she frequented fried chicken and hamburger franchises so much that the managers and salespeople knew her by name. When questioned about whether or not she planned on making changes in her daughter’s diet, Tiffany’s mother emphatically stated, “I do not plan on making any changes to Tiffany’s diet. She’s my kid, and I call the shots about what she eats. Fast food tastes better than the stuff you’re proposing. I know she’s bigger than many of her classmates, but at least she’s happy. All of you doctors are rich anyway, and you think I can afford all that stuff you’re telling me to feed my child.”
Since the mother has been the primary contributor to this patient’s learned behavior (poor dietary choices and sedentary lifestyle), can this be seen as medical maltreatment? Should the physician ask for a child neglect ruling and advise authorities to speak with child protective services for Tiffany?
case_rewrite:
Tiffany, a 9-year-old girl, is morbidly obese, registering above the 150th percentile for weight in her age group and carrying a BMI of 35. Her pediatrician referred her to a pediatric obesity clinic after observing a steady and significant weight increase over the past three years. This escalation in Tiffany’s weight has led to complications including impaired fasting glucose and elevated cholesterol levels. Concerned by the seriousness of her condition, her pediatrician determined that Tiffany needed a more intensive intervention.

At the pediatric obesity clinic, the physician took a thorough history and discovered that Tiffany’s home life offered little encouragement for physical activity. Instead, sedentary behaviors prevailed, and fast food played a central role in the family’s diet. Tiffany’s mother spoke with pride about their frequent visits to local fried chicken and hamburger outlets, even noting that the staff recognized her by name. When the physician broached the topic of dietary changes, Tiffany’s mother responded firmly: “I do not plan on making any changes to Tiffany’s diet. She’s my kid, and I call the shots about what she eats. Fast food tastes better than the stuff you’re proposing. I know she’s bigger than many of her classmates, but at least she’s happy. All of you doctors are rich anyway, and you think I can afford all that stuff you’re telling me to feed my child.”

It became evident that Tiffany’s mother was not only dismissive of medical advice but also played a central role in shaping Tiffany’s unhealthy eating patterns and inactive lifestyle. Given that Tiffany now suffered from serious but potentially reversible health problems due to her obesity, the physician faced a difficult decision: Should this be considered medical neglect? Should the physician seek a child neglect ruling and recommend that child protective services become involved in Tiffany’s case?

ethical_issues.question: Does Pediatric Obesity Constitute Child Neglect, Especially When Caregivers Fail to Seek or Provide Necessary Medical Care?
keypoints: Childhood obesity itself, although it may be severe, typically does not constitute “serious and imminent harm,” and therefore obesity alone is insufficient to establish child neglect.

When childhood obesity results in reversible yet serious comorbid conditions (such as severe obstructive sleep apnea, uncontrolled type 2 diabetes, or progressive fatty liver disease), and the caregiver fails to provide or seek necessary medical care, this may constitute medical neglect.

Physicians should report medical neglect only when the following conditions are met: a high likelihood of serious and imminent harm; a reasonable expectation that available interventions will result in effective treatment; and the absence of feasible alternative solutions.

Socioeconomic factors such as poverty, lack of access to healthy foods, and inadequate opportunities for physical activity may exacerbate childhood obesity; however, when a child faces a risk of serious and imminent harm, the state and healthcare professionals retain an obligation to protect the child.

ethical_issues.question: Should physicians request a child neglect ruling and recommend involvement of child protective services when pediatric obesity is accompanied by serious comorbid conditions?
keypoints: Physicians should first recommend nutritional, exercise, and behavioral interventions, and refer to specialized professionals to ensure that reporting medical neglect is reserved as a measure of last resort.

Physicians should consider seeking a child neglect ruling and advising child protective services to intervene when pediatric obesity is accompanied by serious comorbidities and there is a high risk of serious and imminent harm.

The criteria for reporting medical neglect include: a high likelihood of serious and imminent harm; a reasonable expectation that available interventions will result in effective treatment; and the absence of alternative options to address the problem.

Both the state and physicians have an obligation to protect children from serious harm, even when factors such as poverty, lack of affordable healthy food, and inadequate space for physical activity contribute to severe pediatric obesity.

ethical_issues.question: In cases of pediatric obesity, should physicians exhaust alternative options, including behavioral modification and involvement of social services, before the condition progresses to the point where mandatory reporting is required?
keypoints: Physicians have an obligation to actively intervene in pediatric obesity, exhaust all alternative options, and take timely action to ensure that comprehensive interventions are implemented before the condition progresses to the threshold requiring mandatory reporting.
The primary step is to establish a trusting relationship with the parent, avoiding excessive lecturing or information overload, in order to facilitate effective communication and cooperation.

The initial goal should be to maintain the current weight, gradually guiding the parent and child to improve dietary patterns and increase physical activity, while proposing practical and feasible strategies tailored to the family’s circumstances.
Multidisciplinary professional resources, such as nutrition counseling and health psychologists, should be utilized to provide individualized support and behavioral modification interventions.

Local social service agencies should be involved as early as possible, leveraging their expertise in comprehensive family assessment and needs identification to complement the work of the medical team and strengthen attention and support for the child’s health status.

Ongoing follow-up and adjustment of intervention plans are necessary to address difficulties in accessing healthy food and limitations in the physical activity environment, thereby promoting improvement in the child’s health status.

Only when all intervention measures have failed and the child faces a risk of serious and imminent health harm should the physician consider reporting medical neglect and initiating child protection procedures.

title: Pimping: Report or Do Nothing?
case:
Mark shuffled behind Dr. Powell, furiously writing out the LaFarge equation for estimating oxygen consumption on his clipboard. As usual, he had embarrassed himself while presenting the previous patient after forgetting to do the basic cardiac calculations before rounds. He promised himself he wouldn’t forget it again.
Mark’s pediatric cardiology clerkship, now only in its second week, had been the worst of his clinical years. Cardiology had never been his strong suit. During the first week, Mark’s struggle with interpreting blood gas results and repeated confusion of VSD with ASD had made it apparent that he was the weakest of the five clerkship students.
Today, however, none of his peers were having luck with Dr. Powell’s relentless pimping. Dr. Powell was a decent teacher, though his reliance on that particular teaching tool made every morning miserable.
“Mark, maybe you can do better than your colleague.” Mark snapped to attention. Dr. Powell had begun questioning the group about the next patient. Mark was standing second in the line of students outside the room. His colleague to the left of him looked distraught, fumbling in his notes for the answer to the question he just missed. “Based on the echocardiogram, what kind of congenital defect should we be looking for?”
“Uh,” Mark stuttered. He had viewed the imaging study for this patient before rounds this morning, but he was always terrible at septal defects. “Is it an ASD?” Mark answered tenuously.
“Jesus, Mark, have you learned anything in school?” Dr. Powell barked at the top of his lungs. “I can’t believe you’re even in the same class as these guys! At this rate, the only good thing I’ll be able to do is fail you from this clerkship. Go the library and do not come back until you know what you’re talking about.”
Mark was stunned. Hadn’t the other guy missed the same question? Out of the corner of his eye, Mark saw his colleague’s shocked expression turn into steeled discomfort as he suddenly faced forward. Dr. Powell stared at Mark expectantly. Taking the hint, Mark turned away and started towards the end of the hall as the pimping continued behind him.
Nurses at the charge station at the end of the hallway looked at him sympathetically; they had heard the tirade all the way at the end of the ward. Staring dejectedly at his feet and hating every stupid neuron in his head, Mark literally ran into his colleague Jarna on her pediatrics rotation, tripping over her neon orange clogs.
She put her hand on his shoulder, looked at him straight in the eye, and said, “Suck it up, dude. This is medical school.”
case_rewrite:
Mark trudged behind Dr. Powell, scribbling the LaFarge equation for oxygen consumption on his clipboard, determined not to repeat yesterday’s mistake. He still cringed at how he had floundered during rounds, forgetting basic cardiac calculations and earning a pointed glare from Dr. Powell. This pediatric cardiology clerkship, now in his second week, felt like the hardest stretch of his clinical training. Cardiology had always been his weak spot. In the first week alone, Mark had muddled through blood gas interpretations and mixed up VSDs with ASDs so often that it was clear to everyone—himself included—that he was struggling more than any other student in the group.

But today, Mark wasn’t alone in feeling under siege. None of the five clerks seemed able to handle Dr. Powell’s barrage of rapid-fire questions. Dr. Powell was known for his tough teaching style, and while he was a decent educator, his relentless use of “pimping” made every morning feel like a trial by fire.

“Mark, maybe you can do better than your colleague,” Dr. Powell suddenly called out, his voice slicing through the awkward silence. Mark’s attention snapped back to the group. He stood second in line outside the patient’s room, a step behind his classmate, who was fumbling through his notes after missing a question. “Based on the echocardiogram, what congenital defect are we looking for?” Dr. Powell pressed.

Mark hesitated. He had reviewed the patient’s imaging earlier that morning, but septal defects always tangled him up. “Um, is it an ASD?” he offered, voice uncertain.

Dr. Powell’s reaction was swift and loud. “Jesus, Mark, have you learned anything in school?” he shouted, his tone echoing down the hall. “I can’t believe you’re even in the same class as these guys! At this rate, the only good thing I’ll do is fail you from this clerkship. Go to the library and don’t come back until you know what you’re talking about.”

Mark stood frozen in disbelief. Hadn’t his classmate just missed the same question? He caught a glimpse of his colleague’s face—shocked, then quickly hardening into a mask of discomfort as he turned forward. Dr. Powell stared at Mark, making it clear he expected him to leave. Defeated, Mark turned away and trudged down the hallway, the sound of Dr. Powell’s questioning resuming behind him.

Near the nurses’ station, Mark could feel sympathetic eyes following him—the nurses had heard every word of Dr. Powell’s tirade. Staring at the floor, feeling every bit a failure, he rounded the corner and nearly collided with his friend Jarna, who was on her own pediatrics rotation, her neon orange clogs impossible to miss.

Jarna steadied him with a hand on his shoulder, looked him straight in the eye, and said, “Suck it up, dude. This is medical school.”

ethical_issues.question: Should Mark report Dr. Powell’s persistent questioning style (“pimping”) if he believes it undermines student well-being and human dignity?
keypoints: Reporting Dr. Powell’s inappropriate verbal abuse contributes to upholding the human dignity of medical students and promotes accountability of attending physicians for their teaching conduct.

From the perspective of practical consequences, Mark’s report may not necessarily result in a worse grade; rather, it could prompt the academic administration to revoke Dr. Powell’s evaluation privileges, thereby alleviating Mark’s concerns about grade impairment.

If Dr. Powell’s behavior stems solely from neglecting evidence-based teaching methods, reporting him may facilitate improvements in his instructional approach, benefiting both future students and the physician himself.

If Dr. Powell acts with malicious intent or exhibits character flaws, early reporting and removal from his teaching position is more conducive to protecting student rights and maintaining a healthy educational environment.
Mark should weigh his personal short-term interests against long-term values and choose actions that contribute to fostering a respectful and just teaching environment.

tags: Disparities in health and health care/Race and ethnicity
title: Why It’s Unjust to Expect Location-Specific, Language-Specific, or Population-Specific Service from Students with Underrepresented Minority or Low-Income Backgrounds
case:
Amanda is a second-year medical student at a private Midwestern medical school, which she is able to attend thanks to an institutional scholarship and federal financial aid. She has been seriously engaged with campaigns on campus for health equity and social justice in the community and in the country at large. Amanda grew up in a family with mixed Native American and Latin American roots and was a first-generation college graduate in her family; thus, issues of access to education and health care are very important to her.
Amanda grew up speaking Spanish fluently and studied medical Chinese in her first year of medical school. She has used her language skills in a medical student-run clinic that provides free basic clinical services to those with limited English proficiency (LEP), which includes Spanish and Chinese speakers. As a second-year medical student, she has begun thinking about clinical years and plans for a successful residency match. During her recent visit with her family over Christmas, her parents and maternal grandmother expressed their pride in her accomplishments and their desire that she match into a competitive specialty and residency program.
At school, she is having a discussion with friends about their current career interests. When she expresses her anxieties about what it takes to match into a competitive specialty in an elite residency program, her friends express surprise. “I thought since you were so passionate about social justice, you’d be more interested in working with minority populations back home.” Others concur and express the opinion that, as someone with the cultural competencies and language skills to work with immigrants in her home state, she has a responsibility to utilize her skills for LEP populations. She wonders what to say.
case_rewrite:
Amanda, a second-year medical student at a private medical school in the Midwest, attends on the strength of both an institutional scholarship and federal financial aid. Deeply committed to health equity and social justice, Amanda has become an active voice in campus movements for accessible healthcare and education, both locally and nationally. Her personal history—growing up in a family with mixed Native American and Latin American heritage and being the first in her family to graduate from college—has made these issues profoundly important to her.

Fluent in Spanish from childhood, Amanda also began learning medical Chinese during her first year of medical school. She often puts these language skills to use at a student-run clinic that offers free basic healthcare to individuals with limited English proficiency, serving both Spanish- and Chinese-speaking patients. Now, as she looks ahead to her clinical years and starts considering her options for residency, Amanda feels the weight of future decisions. During a recent holiday visit, her parents and maternal grandmother expressed their pride in her progress and their strong hope that she will match into a highly competitive specialty and residency program.

Back at school, Amanda finds herself engaged in a conversation with friends about their career plans and aspirations. She shares her anxiety about the challenges of matching into a top-tier specialty at a prestigious residency. Her friends express surprise, with one remarking, “Given how passionate you are about social justice, I thought you’d want to work with minority communities back home.” Others agree, suggesting that, because of her cultural background and language abilities, Amanda has a responsibility to use her skills for the benefit of immigrant and LEP populations in her home state. As Amanda listens, she is left wondering how to respond to these expectations.
