keypoints: Medical decision-making should prioritize the athlete’s long-term health, ensuring a safe and evidence-based return to play.
The physician should communicate clearly with the athlete regarding the dual goals of facilitating a safe return to sports participation and preventing or managing long-term health risks.
A staged return-to-play plan should be developed, with progressive goals to help the athlete understand and accept the appropriate timing for resuming participation.
Treatment of sports injuries must address both physiological recovery and the impact on other aspects of the athlete’s life, with attention to overall well-being.

Through multidisciplinary collaboration and effective communication, a safe return-to-play pathway can be scientifically planned, and ethical issues in sports injury medical decision-making can be appropriately managed.

title: How to Talk with Patients about Incarceration and Health
case:
Dr. Wen works at a busy primary care practice in an urban community health center. Today he sees that Luke, a 43-year-old man with a diagnosis of hypertension and major depression, is scheduled for a visit. Luke has missed three visits in the last four months without warning and has not refilled his lisinopril or fluoxetine prescriptions during this time. Dr. Wen mentions this to his medical assistant, Jason, and expresses concern about Luke’s blood pressure. Jason happens to live down the street from Luke and tells Dr. Wen, “I heard Luke’s been locked up for the past couple of months because of a robbery.”
When Luke finally arrives for an appointment, Jason reports his blood pressure is 141/87. When asked by Dr. Wen about his medication supply, Luke states he has been taking lisinopril and fluoxetine as directed until last week, when “my prescription ran out.” Aware of the many health risks associated with incarceration—including loss of health insurance, loss of social supports, difficulty obtaining employment upon reentry, and higher rates of chronic disease—Dr. Wen asks Luke if he has recently been incarcerated or detained. Luke looks surprised and then becomes irate, yelling, “That’s none of your business! Why are you asking about things that have nothing to do with you?”
case_rewrite:
Dr. Wen works at a busy primary care clinic in an urban community health center. One afternoon, he notes that Luke, a 43-year-old man diagnosed with hypertension and major depressive disorder, is scheduled for an appointment. Over the past four months, Luke has missed three scheduled visits without prior notice and has failed to refill his prescriptions for lisinopril and fluoxetine during this period. Dr. Wen shares his concern about Luke’s blood pressure control with his medical assistant, Jason. Jason, who lives nearby, responds, “I heard Luke’s been locked up for the last couple of months because of a robbery.”

When Luke eventually arrives for his appointment, Jason records a blood pressure of 141/87. During the visit, Dr. Wen asks Luke about his current medication supply. Luke replies that he took his lisinopril and fluoxetine as prescribed until last week, when he ran out of medication. Cognizant of the many health risks linked to incarceration—such as loss of health insurance, diminished social support, employment challenges upon release, and increased burden of chronic illness—Dr. Wen gently asks Luke whether he has recently been incarcerated or detained. Luke initially looks taken aback and then becomes visibly upset, raising his voice: “That’s none of your business! Why are you asking about things that have nothing to do with you?”

ethical_issues.question: When documenting a patient’s social history, how can clinicians avoid triggering stigma and unconscious bias while adhering to the HIPAA Privacy Rule’s “minimum necessary” standard of disclosure?
keypoints: Adhere to the “minimum necessary” disclosure standard under the HIPAA Privacy Rule, ensuring that only social information directly relevant to clinical care is collected, thereby maximizing the protection of patient privacy.

Utilize systematic and multidimensional tools such as structural vulnerability checklists, implemented by a multidisciplinary team (including social workers, medical assistants, nurses, and physicians), to comprehensively assess patients’ social determinants of health. This approach avoids singularly focusing on incarceration history and helps reduce stigma and unconscious bias.

Avoid inquiring about or documenting sensitive details such as the reasons for incarceration, in order to prevent labeling or targeting of patients and to maintain trust and respect within the patient-physician relationship.
When documenting social history, use neutral and respectful language, and avoid terminology that may trigger bias or reinforce negative stereotypes.
Communicate transparently with patients about the purpose and use of collecting social history, in order to enhance patient understanding and cooperation and to reduce resistance or discomfort.

ethical_issues.question: Does obtaining information about a patient’s social background from other individuals without the patient’s authorization constitute a violation of the patient’s right to privacy?
keypoints: Obtaining a patient’s social background information through informal channels (such as a medical assistant or community acquaintances) without the patient’s authorization constitutes a violation of the patient’s right to privacy.
Physicians should prioritize obtaining sensitive information, such as a history of incarceration, directly from the patient in order to respect the patient’s autonomy and right to privacy.

When a physician initiates clinical care based on informal information provided by an assistant, it is necessary to balance ethical risks with clinical needs, ensuring that both the means of obtaining information are appropriate and the clinical objectives are justified.

ethical_issues.question: Should physicians proactively inquire about a patient’s history of incarceration, even if doing so might offend the patient or elicit social stigma?
keypoints: The physician inquires about the patient’s history of incarceration in order to gain a comprehensive understanding of the patient’s health status, as incarceration can have multiple direct and indirect effects on both physical and mental health.

Utilizing tools such as a structural vulnerability screening checklist allows for a systematic assessment of patients’ social vulnerabilities—including a history of incarceration—without making patients feel singled out or labeled.

In communities with high rates of incarceration, physicians can introduce the topic of incarceration history through routine, non-targeted questioning and by explaining its potential impact on health, thereby reducing patients’ defensiveness.

When patients respond negatively to questions about incarceration history, physicians should begin by apologizing and expressing understanding and respect, then clarify the medical relevance of the inquiry in order to de-escalate conflict and rebuild trust.

Physicians should strive to create an inclusive and safe clinical environment, normalize discussions of incarceration history in a nonjudgmental manner, respect patients’ choices regarding disclosure, and foster longitudinal trust within the patient-physician relationship.

tags: Diagnosis/Patient request for a specific test or procedure
title: Grow a Spine, Have a Heart: Responding to Patient Requests for Marginally Beneficial Care
case:
Dr. Perry is a primary care physician in a busy urban clinic in East Harlem in New York City. He is already behind schedule and has been somewhat apprehensive about seeing his next patient, 42-year-old Ms. Hollowell, whose medical problems are prediabetes and obesity. Ms. Hollowell comes to clinic for both scheduled visits and urgent care walk-ins. Dr. Perry has seen her perhaps once a month for the past several years.
Today, she asks Dr. Perry for a referral for a mammogram. She is concerned that breast cancer may run in her family. An aunt of hers had breast cancer in her 60s, and two years before Ms. Hollowell had convinced Dr. Perry that she should get a “baseline” mammogram. That test was indeterminate, and she then was sent for a right breast ultrasound and diagnostic mammogram, which were both negative.
Dr. Perry believes that, because Ms. Hollowell is between 40 and 50 and is in a low-risk group based on the new screening guidelines, a mammogram is unnecessary at this time. Still, he recognizes that Ms. Hollowell is anxious and wants to get a mammogram to “make sure everything is alright.” He explains the risks of false positives again, but Ms. Hollowell points out the “close call” and “cancer scare” she had two years ago and her desire to be reassured. He fears that not referring her will hurt the therapeutic relationship that has been built over the years. Perhaps she will seek out another physician if he refuses her request for a referral. Dr. Perry quickly checks the computer and sees that he is running behind schedule; there are three other patients waiting to be seen.
case_rewrite:
Dr. Perry, a primary care physician at a bustling clinic in East Harlem, New York City, was running behind schedule on a busy afternoon. He felt a tinge of apprehension as he prepared for his next appointment with Ms. Hollowell, a 42-year-old patient he had come to know well over several years. Ms. Hollowell, whose medical history includes prediabetes and obesity, frequently visited the clinic—sometimes for routine appointments, other times for urgent concerns. On average, Dr. Perry saw her about once a month.

Today, Ms. Hollowell sat across from Dr. Perry with a specific request: she wanted a referral for a mammogram. She explained her rising anxiety about breast cancer, mentioning that an aunt had been diagnosed in her 60s. Two years earlier, Ms. Hollowell had persuaded Dr. Perry to order a “baseline” mammogram for her, which had produced indeterminate results. This led to a follow-up right breast ultrasound and a diagnostic mammogram, both of which ultimately were negative.

Now, Ms. Hollowell was seeking additional reassurance. Dr. Perry, recalling the new screening guidelines, knew that women in Ms. Hollowell’s age group—between 40 and 50—who are considered low-risk do not require routine mammography at this time. He tried to explain again the potential downsides, such as the risk of false positives, but Ms. Hollowell persisted, referencing her prior “close call” and the “cancer scare” that still lingered in her mind. She expressed a strong desire for the peace of mind that another mammogram might bring.

Dr. Perry hesitated. He was aware that denying Ms. Hollowell’s request could strain the trusting relationship they had built over years. He worried she might seek out another doctor if he refused her, jeopardizing continuity of care. Yet, he also recognized his duty to act in her best clinical interests and avoid unnecessary testing. A quick glance at the computer screen reminded him that three more patients were already waiting. The pressure of time, the constraints of the healthcare system, and the complexities of insurance coverage all weighed on his mind as he considered how best to respond to Ms. Hollowell’s request.

ethical_issues.question: In the U.S. healthcare system, should physicians consider cost factors in clinical decision-making to limit overtreatment?
keypoints: In the U.S. healthcare system, the physician’s role in limiting overtreatment is highly controversial, as it may create conflicts between patient interests and resource allocation, thereby compromising the physician’s duty as a patient advocate.

The majority of U.S. physicians believe that the responsibility for limiting access to medical services and rationing resources should be shared among insurers, healthcare systems, the legal framework, and patients, rather than physicians directly considering cost factors in clinical decision-making.

The adoption of an administrative gatekeeping mechanism is recommended, whereby physicians implement fair resource allocation policies established at higher levels within the healthcare system, while being prohibited from incorporating cost considerations into individual clinical decisions, thus ensuring focus on the patient’s best interests.

Physicians should concentrate on the welfare of the individual patient and be willing to refuse unnecessary medical services based solely on clinical best interests, rather than acting as self-appointed negative gatekeepers.

The medical profession collectively bears a social responsibility to actively engage in professional discourse and policy development at the systemic level, promoting the establishment of a fair, just, and effective system for healthcare resource allocation.

ethical_issues.question: How should physicians balance the patient’s best interests with adherence to insurance policies when refusing a patient’s request for diagnostic testing?
keypoints: Physicians should prioritize the individual welfare of the patient and, based on the principle of best interests, be willing to refuse patient requests for tests when clinically appropriate.

Physicians must seek a balance between respecting patient autonomy and the principles of beneficence and nonmaleficence, guiding patients to understand the risks and benefits of medical interventions in a reasonable manner.

Physicians should not serve as instruments to compensate for inequities in insurance policies and societal resource allocation; their clinical decisions ought to remain independent of systemic policy deficiencies.
When physicians refuse to provide medically necessary services covered by insurance, they must invoke ethical principles that go beyond mere considerations of resource allocation.

tags: Patient-clinician relationship/Patients’ responsibility for their health
title: Informing Adolescents of Perinatally Contracted HIV
case:
Dr. Patel was a pediatrics resident on his ambulatory rotation. Picking up the chart of 15-year-old Julio, he read a lengthy discussion between the previous resident, Dr. O’Neill, and Julio’s mother. Dr. O’Neill reported that as she entered the patient’s room, Julio’s mother had pulled him aside to inform him that Julio and she were HIV-positive. Julio had contracted HIV at birth from her but was unaware of her status or his own. His mother had gone on to tell Dr. O’Neill that Julio had never been hospitalized and had never required antiretroviral therapy. She explained that when he was younger, he was always healthy, so she thought there was no reason for him to know that he carried the virus. She then informed Dr. O’Neill that Julio’s previous pediatrician had respected her wishes not to inform him of his status and she expected this behavior to continue at this new clinic.
Dr. O’Neill reported that she had urged Julio’s mother to change her mind. “You need to tell him about his HIV status. He is old enough to understand now, and I would strongly encourage you to sit down with him and have a discussion about it. The longer you wait, the harder it is going to be to tell him you have been hiding something like this from him for so long.”
The mother had replied that Julio was healthy and happy and had lots of friends at school. As long as he was not ill, she felt no reason for him to think he was different from other kids or to worry about getting sick.
From the documentation, Dr. O’Neill and her supervising attending physicians strongly encouraged Julio’s mother to reconsider her decision. Fearing that she would avoid medical care for her son altogether, however, they decided to respect her wishes until a relationship could be built among the patient, his mother, and the clinic members.
Dr. Patel reviewed Julio’s blood work. His CD4 count was suitably high, so he would not require medications. Entering the patient’s room he was relieved to see that Julio’s mother was not with him on this visit. Dr. Patel encountered a healthy-appearing teenage boy who seemed more concerned about whether he should be using Proactive for his facial acne than about why he was at the doctor’s office again and what his blood work had demonstrated. Dr. Patel was relieved that he would not have to deal with all this HIV business on this visit. Wrapping up, he asked Julio if there was anything else he wanted to discuss.
“Um, by the way doc, I didn’t wanna bring this up last time, because the doctor was a girl and all, but uh, you see, me and my girlfriend, we have been dating for almost a year,” Julio said. “We’ve been getting really serious, and we’ve been talking about having sex. I know that we need to use condoms and I know how to use them from that class we took in school. And my girlfriend, she was gonna talk to her doctor about getting birth control pills. We are both virgins, so I’m not worried about STDs. I was wondering if there is anything else that I should know, you know, to keep us both safe?”
Dr. Patel swallowed hard, he said he needed to speak to his attending and then he would come back and address all of Julio’s questions at once. As he left the room, Dr. Patel knew there was definitely something else Julio needed to know.
case_rewrite:
Dr. Patel, a pediatrics resident on his ambulatory rotation, picked up the chart for his next patient, 15-year-old Julio. The notes from the previous resident, Dr. O’Neill, caught his attention—a detailed account of a private conversation with Julio’s mother. According to the record, as Dr. O’Neill prepared to enter the exam room during a prior visit, Julio’s mother had intercepted her just outside. In a hushed voice, she revealed that both she and Julio were HIV-positive. Julio, she explained, had contracted HIV at birth from her, but he remained completely unaware of both his own status and hers.

Julio’s mother had gone on to say that he had never been hospitalized or needed antiretroviral medication. He’d always been healthy, so, in her view, there was no reason for him to know anything different about himself. She insisted that she wanted to protect him from feeling different or worrying unnecessarily. She also added that Julio’s previous pediatrician had agreed to keep the diagnosis secret from him, and she expected the same discretion from this new clinic.

Dr. O’Neill had documented her response, urging Julio’s mother to reconsider. “He’s old enough to understand his diagnosis now,” she had said. “It’s important that you tell him. The longer you wait, the harder it will be for him to process that you kept this from him for so long.” Still, Julio’s mother remained firm, arguing that since Julio was healthy, happy, and thriving socially, she saw no benefit in telling him something that might upset or isolate him.

The attending physician and Dr. O’Neill had both encouraged Julio’s mother to rethink her decision. However, they worried that pushing too hard might cause her to avoid seeking medical care for Julio altogether. For the time being, they agreed to respect her wishes, hoping to build enough trust with the family to eventually revisit the topic.

Now, Dr. Patel reviewed Julio’s latest lab results. His CD4 count was healthy, and there was no indication that he needed to start medications. As Dr. Patel entered the exam room, he noted that Julio’s mother was absent—a small relief. He found a healthy-looking teenage boy more preoccupied with the state of his skin than the blood work Dr. Patel had just reviewed. The appointment seemed routine, and Dr. Patel felt relieved that he wouldn’t have to address the sensitive matter of HIV disclosure that day.

As the visit was wrapping up, Dr. Patel asked, “Anything else you want to talk about?”

Julio hesitated for a moment, then spoke up. “Um, actually, yeah, Doc. I didn’t really want to mention this last time, because the doctor was a woman, but, uh, me and my girlfriend—we’ve been together almost a year. Things are getting serious, and we’ve been talking about having sex. I know we should use condoms, and I learned how from health class at school. My girlfriend’s thinking about going on the pill, too. We’re both virgins, so I’m not worried about STDs. But I wanted to ask if there’s anything else I should know, you know, to keep us both safe?”

Dr. Patel felt a knot form in his stomach. He told Julio he needed to check in with his attending and would return to answer all of his questions at once. As Dr. Patel left the room, he realized there was something vitally important that Julio needed to know.

ethical_issues.question: Does the physician have a responsibility to disclose an adolescent patient’s HIV status to the patient, even if the patient is currently unaware of their diagnosis, in order to protect their health and safety?
keypoints: Physicians have a responsibility to inform adolescent patients of their HIV status in order to safeguard their health and safety. The American Academy of Pediatrics strongly recommends disclosure of HIV infection to adolescents; all adolescents should be aware of their own status and be fully informed about all relevant health information, including matters related to sexual behavior.

Timely disclosure of HIV status helps to prevent social isolation, enhance coping skills, reduce anxiety, maintain trust, and lower the risk of depression, thereby promoting the psychosocial well-being of the patient.

Physicians should encourage adolescents to involve their parents in their care, respect family relationships and support systems, and foster trust and collaboration among the physician, patient, and parents.

When an adolescent intends to engage in sexual activity with a partner presumed to be HIV-negative, the physician must prioritize the adolescent’s best interests. If necessary, the physician should proactively assess the patient’s understanding of their condition and ensure disclosure of the HIV status, even if this goes against parental wishes, in order to protect the health and safety of both the patient and their potential partner.

Physicians should actively seek multiple approaches to protect the health and safety of the patient and their partner, and should not rely solely on communication with the guardian. Disclosure strategies should be flexibly tailored to the patient’s level of maturity and specific circumstances.

ethical_issues.question: In the case of an adolescent patient who is planning to become sexually active, should the physician prioritize disclosing the patient’s HIV status in order to protect the health and safety of their partner?
keypoints: The patient’s intention to initiate sexual activity represents a critical turning point, rendering the “disclosure at an appropriate time” strategy untenable; the physician must now prioritize disclosure of the diagnosis in order to protect the health and safety of the adolescent patient and his partner.

The physician should actively seek effective means to safeguard Julio and his potential partner’s health, while respecting the patient’s privacy and right to know, and need not be limited to direct discussion with the mother.

The physician should offer confidential HIV testing services to Julio and his partner, and provide comprehensive education regarding all modes of transmission, including sexual, perinatal, and blood-borne routes.
Regardless of whether Julio and his partner choose to undergo HIV testing, the physician should provide guidance on safer sexual practices and emphasize the importance of correct condom use.

The physician should balance the establishment of a trusting physician-patient relationship with the obligation to disclose, and gradually guide the patient and his guardian toward an accurate understanding and appropriate response to the HIV diagnosis.

title: What Should Physicians and Chaplains Do When a Patient Believes God Wants Him to Suffer?
case:
Mr. L is a 47-year-old father of 2 who has a history of alcohol abuse but has been sober for over a year. He was admitted from the emergency department, where he presented earlier this morning with acute abdominal pain. He was diagnosed with pancreatitis and biliary colic, indicating the need for a cholecystectomy (a laparoscopic procedure to remove the gallbladder to prevent gall stones, pain, and infection). However, before the procedure could take place, Mr. L stated that he did not want pain medication after the surgery because, as he said, “God wants me to be in pain.” The medical team, unsure how to proceed, delayed the surgery.
Dr. J, a fourth-year surgery resident, met with Mr. L to discuss his request and quickly reach a resolution, as the medical team did not want to delay the procedure for more than 24 hours. After Mr. L explained why he did not want pain medication, Dr. J stated, “You are going to feel a lot of pain after this surgery. Sometimes the pain is so extreme that patients have difficulty breathing. So the pain medication helps you be able to take full breaths, which reduces the likelihood of getting pneumonia.” Dr. J then asked Mr. L if he would be willing to speak with a chaplain about his ideas of what God wants for him, and Mr. L agreed.
Dr. J consulted with the chaplain on call, Mr. K, and explained Mr. L’s case. “We can’t, in good conscience, not give him pain medication,” she said. “It’s just bad care. I respect his beliefs, but I can’t be forced to give him what I know to be bad care because of his beliefs. We need to manage the pain to help him heal, if not to be compassionate.” Mr. K suggested, “I’ll speak with him to get a better understanding of his spiritual concerns. Why don’t we talk after I meet with him?”
Mr. K visited Mr. L. They spent some time getting to know each other and, eventually, Mr. K asked, “So would you tell me more about why you think God wants you to be in pain after your surgery?” Mr. L nodded his head and lifted his hand. “I’ve done a lot of wrong in my life and hurt a lot of people. I haven’t been a good father to my kids. And from the way I see it, God wants me to be in pain—God wants me to suffer through this so I can atone for some of my sins. And God’s right—I don’t deserve the pain meds and I don’t want the pain meds.”
Dr. J and Mr. K now meet and consider how to proceed.
case_rewrite:
Mr. L, a 47-year-old father of two, has a history of alcohol abuse but has remained sober for over a year. Earlier this morning, he arrived at the emergency department with severe abdominal pain and was subsequently diagnosed with pancreatitis and biliary colic. The surgical team determined that Mr. L would need a laparoscopic cholecystectomy to remove his gallbladder and prevent further pain, gallstone formation, and infection. However, before the operation could proceed, Mr. L informed the team that he did not want to receive any pain medication after the surgery, explaining, “God wants me to be in pain.” Uncertain about how to honor Mr. L’s wishes while also upholding their duty to provide good care, the medical team decided to postpone the surgery.

Dr. J, a fourth-year surgical resident, met with Mr. L to discuss his request further. Conscious that the surgery should not be delayed for more than 24 hours, Dr. J sought to quickly reach an understanding. As they spoke, Mr. L reiterated his conviction, and Dr. J explained, “After this surgery, you’re likely to experience significant pain. Sometimes, the pain can be so intense that patients have trouble breathing deeply, which increases their risk of developing pneumonia. Pain medication not only helps with comfort but also makes it easier to recover safely.” Dr. J then asked if Mr. L would be open to speaking with a chaplain about his beliefs and what he thought God wanted for him. Mr. L agreed.

Dr. J contacted the on-call chaplain, Mr. K, and described the situation: “We can’t, in good conscience, withhold pain medication,” she said. “That would be poor care. I genuinely respect Mr. L’s beliefs, but I don’t think I can agree to a plan that leaves him in unnecessary pain, especially if it might compromise his recovery. At the same time, I want to honor his religious perspective.” Mr. K suggested, “I’ll meet with Mr. L to better understand his spiritual concerns. Let’s reconvene after I’ve spoken with him.”

Mr. K visited Mr. L in his hospital room. They spent time getting acquainted, and eventually, Mr. K gently asked, “Could you help me understand why you believe God wants you to be in pain after your surgery?” Mr. L nodded solemnly and raised his hand. “I’ve made a lot of mistakes in my life. I’ve hurt people, including my own kids. I haven’t been the father I should have been. In my mind, God wants me to go through this pain as a way to atone for my sins. I don’t deserve pain medication, and I don’t want it. This is something I need to face.”

After this conversation, Dr. J and Mr. K met to discuss how best to move forward in caring for Mr. L, weighing his religious convictions against their responsibility to ensure his health and well-being.

ethical_issues.question: How should physicians balance respecting patient autonomy and upholding their commitment to the patient’s health when confronted with a patient’s refusal of necessary pain medication based on religious beliefs?
keypoints: Physicians must understand the patient’s religious reasoning and seek a balance between respecting the patient’s right to refuse and upholding their commitment to health, with the commitment to health delineating the boundaries of acceptable medical decisions.

Physicians have an obligation to respectfully challenge patients’ refusals of medically necessary care and to collaboratively explore courses of action that honor the patient’s religious concerns, thereby demonstrating sincere respect for the patient’s faith.

If withholding pain medication would unduly compromise the patient’s health or the likelihood of surgical success, the physician should refuse this course of action; conversely, if the risks are manageable, the physician should respect the patient’s religiously informed wishes.

Physicians must humbly acknowledge the limits of their knowledge and expertise and proactively seek the assistance of chaplains or religious leaders to better respect and respond to the patient’s religious and spiritual beliefs.

ethical_issues.question: How should chaplains position their role within the healthcare team to provide effective spiritual care without compromising the independence of medical decision-making?
keypoints: The chaplain’s primary role within the healthcare team is to provide religious and spiritual care to the patient, focusing on supporting the patient’s spiritual needs rather than directly persuading them to accept specific medical interventions.

The chaplain should continuously attend to and seek to understand the patient’s theological understanding of suffering and any changes therein, assisting the patient in exploring the meaning of suffering within their faith framework.

The chaplain may appropriately challenge and guide the patient to reflect on their religious interpretations, exploring diverse theological perspectives on themes such as suffering and atonement within the patient’s religious tradition, thereby fostering the patient’s spiritual growth.

The chaplain must maintain independence and not act as a representative of the medical institution’s predetermined clinical goals, but rather serve as a collaborative member who promotes the patient’s overall well-being.

Although the chaplain and physician have distinct responsibilities, both should share the common goal of respecting and caring for the patient’s physical, mental, and spiritual well-being, working collaboratively to support the patient’s treatment process.

title: Is Parental Smoking Neglect of an Asthmatic Child?
case:
A mother carrying a coughing child walks into the emergency room. She hysterically flags down a triage nurse and tells her that her daughter, Rose, is having trouble breathing. The nurse directs mother and child to a bed in the emergency room cordoned off by a light blue curtain. Less than five minutes later, Tricia, a third-year medical student on her pediatrics rotation, shows up to do a thorough history and physical of the patient. The first thing Tricia notices is that both mother and daughter are saturated in the scent of cigarettes. Upon questioning, the mother admits to smoking two packs a day in the house.
“Have you tried quitting?” Tricia asks.
The mother scowls. “The smoking’s not a problem. I keep all the windows open.” At that moment, her daughter has a severe coughing fit. She scoops Rose into her arms, and rubs soothing circles on her back. “My daughter has asthma. That’s why we’re here,” she tells the student.
Tricia jots a note in the patient’s record and sees Rose has been admitted multiple times in the past for asthma. After flipping through these notes, Tricia sees that the mother has been counseled repeatedly about the need to stop smoking for the sake of Rose’s health. Tricia goes to find her attending and presents Rose’s case, highlighting signs of neglect. She then asks whether or not this would be grounds to notify child protective services.
case_rewrite:
A mother carrying her coughing daughter hurries into the emergency department. She anxiously waves down the triage nurse, her voice trembling as she explains that her daughter, Rose, is struggling to breathe. The nurse quickly ushers them to a bed in the emergency area, separated from the rest of the unit by a light blue curtain. Within minutes, Tricia, a third-year medical student on her pediatrics rotation, arrives to conduct a full history and physical exam.

The heavy scent of cigarette smoke immediately strikes Tricia as she approaches; both mother and daughter’s clothes are saturated with it. During the interview, Tricia gently asks the mother about smoking in the home. The mother admits, “I smoke about two packs a day, but only in the house.”

Tricia asks, “Have you considered trying to quit, or maybe smoking outside instead?”

The mother stiffens, her expression defensive. “I don’t think the smoking’s the problem. I always keep the windows open.” Just then, Rose is racked by a fierce coughing spell. The mother scoops her up, rubbing soothing circles on her back. “She has asthma. That’s why we’re here again,” she says, casting a worried glance at her daughter.

Tricia makes a note in Rose’s chart and reviews her medical history. She notes that Rose has been admitted numerous times before for asthma exacerbations. The records indicate that previous providers have repeatedly counseled the mother about the dangers of smoking around Rose and the urgent need to protect her from secondhand smoke. Despite these conversations, the notes suggest little change in the mother’s behavior.

Tricia seeks out her attending, Dr. W, and presents Rose’s case, emphasizing the repeated hospitalizations and persistent exposure to secondhand smoke at home. She voices her growing concern about whether this ongoing pattern amounts to medical neglect and asks Dr. W if these circumstances might require notifying child protective services.

ethical_issues.question: How can children’s exposure to secondhand smoke be effectively reduced when parents are not ready to quit smoking?
keypoints: Implement gradual measures to reduce children’s exposure to secondhand smoke, respecting parents’ readiness to change and avoiding coercive demands for smoking cessation.
Establish a partnership with smoking parents to enhance communication and trust, thereby facilitating incremental improvements in the home environment.
Advise parents to smoke outdoors whenever possible to minimize the direct impact of indoor smoke on the child.
Emphasize the importance of prohibiting smoking inside vehicles to prevent children’s exposure to secondhand smoke in enclosed spaces.
Encourage smokers to use a designated smoking jacket kept outdoors and to wash their hands after smoking to reduce smoke odor and residue exposure to the child.

ethical_issues.question: Under what circumstances should physicians report parental neglect to child protective services in order to safeguard the child’s safety?
keypoints: If, after comprehensive interventions, the child continues to be harmed by secondhand smoke, physicians have an ethical and legal obligation to report neglect in order to ensure the child’s safety.

When the actions or omissions of parents or caregivers result in serious physical or emotional harm to the child, or present an imminent risk of such harm, physicians are required by law to report to child welfare authorities.

It is necessary to assess whether there is a persistent pattern of neglect, such as repeated noncompliance with medical advice, missed medical appointments, or failure to fill the child’s prescriptions, as this constitutes an important criterion for reporting.

Physicians must weigh the benefits and risks of reporting, with particular emphasis on evaluating the relative harm of secondhand smoke exposure versus the potential adverse effects of removing the child from the family environment on the child’s safety and well-being.

ethical_issues.question: Does parental smoking constitute neglect of a child with asthma?
keypoints: Neglect includes the failure to meet a child’s basic needs and to protect the child from health hazards such as exposure to secondhand smoke.
Exposure to secondhand smoke can trigger or exacerbate asthma in children, seriously compromising their health and safety.
The US Department of Health and Human Services classifies asthmatic children exposed to secondhand smoke as cases of hazard exposure, which may constitute inadequate supervision and neglect.
In some states, parental failure to follow medical advice—such as recommendations to quit smoking—is considered medical neglect.

The Children and Families Safe Act of 2003 defines child maltreatment as any act or failure to act by a parent or caregiver, including medical neglect, that results in serious physical or emotional harm or presents an imminent risk of serious harm.

A mother’s persistent smoking and failure to take effective measures to quit, despite being aware of the harm of secondhand smoke to a child with asthma, constitutes neglect of the child’s health and safety.

tags: Informed consent/Capacity, competency for making decisions
title: Oncofertility for Adolescents: When Parents and Physicians Disagree about Egg Cryopreservation for a Mature Minor
case:
Evelyn is 15 and was recently diagnosed with a rare small cell cancer of the left ovary. Although she was able to undergo fertility-sparing surgery with preservation of her uterus and right ovary, metastatic disease was found in the para-aortic nodes at the level of the renal vessels. Her treatment will include pelvic irradiation and six cycles of cisplatin, paclitaxel, and etoposide, all known gonadal cytotoxic chemotherapeutic drugs. Her gynecologic oncologist, Dr. Clark, is familiar with the data on adolescent fertility preservation through egg and sperm banking, a concept known as oncofertility.
Because Evelyn is a minor, Dr. Clark approaches the topic of oncofertility with her parents. Evelyn’s mother and father both opposes bringing the topic up with their daughter because they feel she is too young to consider how fertility and infertility would affect the rest of her life and does not have the maturity to make that decision.
Dr. Clark wants to provide the best possible care for Evelyn, and this includes informing her of all the risks and benefits of her treatment. Moreover, he believes that it is his responsibility to present to Evelyn all the possible means of achieving a high quality of life following her treatment. He can’t help but think that one day Evelyn will appreciate the fact that she still has the options to have children from her own eggs, even if she ultimately chooses not to do so. He explains his reasoning to Evelyn’s parents, but they persist in their decision that Evelyn not be offered the intervention. They ask about the egg retrieval process for oncofertility, and Dr. Clark’s answer reinforces their decision. Adding an oocyte retrieval procedure to radiation and chemotherapy is just too much to put Evelyn through, they say.
case_rewrite:
Evelyn, a 15-year-old girl, was recently diagnosed with a rare small cell cancer in her left ovary. She underwent fertility-sparing surgery that preserved her uterus and right ovary, but further evaluation revealed that the cancer had spread to the para-aortic lymph nodes near the renal vessels. Evelyn’s treatment plan includes pelvic radiation and six cycles of chemotherapy with cisplatin, paclitaxel, and etoposide—all of which are known to be toxic to the ovaries and can severely impact future fertility.

Dr. Clark, Evelyn’s gynecologic oncologist, is well-versed in the field of oncofertility, which aims to help adolescent cancer patients preserve their reproductive options through egg or sperm banking. Given Evelyn’s age, Dr. Clark understands the importance of considering her potential future desires regarding having children and wants to ensure that she is informed of all risks and options related to her treatment.

Because Evelyn is a minor, Dr. Clark first discusses the topic of fertility preservation with her parents. However, Evelyn’s mother and father are firmly opposed to introducing the subject to their daughter. They believe Evelyn is too young to understand or weigh the long-term implications of fertility and infertility, and they doubt she possesses the maturity needed to participate in such a decision. They feel that raising the issue would only overwhelm her at a vulnerable time.

Dr. Clark, however, feels a professional and ethical responsibility to make sure Evelyn is fully informed about the impact her treatment may have on her future fertility, as well as the options available to possibly preserve it. He believes that providing Evelyn with this information is essential to supporting her autonomy and ensuring the best possible quality of life after her cancer treatment. Dr. Clark shares his reasoning with Evelyn’s parents, emphasizing that even if Evelyn chooses not to pursue fertility preservation, she may one day appreciate having had the opportunity to make that choice for herself.

Despite Dr. Clark’s efforts to explain the benefits of discussing oncofertility with Evelyn, her parents remain resolute. They ask Dr. Clark to explain the egg retrieval process in detail, and after hearing about what the procedure would entail in the context of Evelyn’s ongoing radiation and chemotherapy, they insist that adding another medical intervention is simply too much for Evelyn to endure. They maintain that the topic should not be brought up with her.

Dr. Clark is left grappling with how best to advocate for Evelyn’s interests, balancing respect for her parents’ wishes with his duty to inform and support his patient in decisions that could significantly affect her future.

ethical_issues.question: In the case of Evelyn, how should healthcare providers ethically navigate conflicts between her parents’ wishes and Evelyn’s emerging autonomy and right to informed assent or consent regarding oncofertility preservation?
keypoints: Physicians should respect the decision-making authority of parents as legal guardians while rationally assessing the maturity and decision-making capacity of the minor patient, acknowledging the principle of the “mature minor” and safeguarding the minor’s right to informed consent.

When parental decisions conflict with the physician’s professional recommendations, a multidisciplinary intervention should be initiated, utilizing family meetings and other formats to thoroughly explore parental concerns and provide expert guidance, with the aim of persuading parents that offering fertility preservation options to the patient is ethically appropriate and beneficial to the patient’s future quality of life.

Patients should be provided with counseling, education, and peer support to help them understand the risks of treatment and the possibilities of fertility preservation, thereby promoting active patient participation in medical decision-making on an informed basis.

ethical_issues.question: When parents’ medical decisions conflict with the physician’s professional recommendations, how should the physician uphold the best interests of the minor patient?
keypoints: Physicians should first thoroughly explore the parents’ concerns and understand the specific reasons behind their opposition to discussing fertility preservation with the minor patient, thereby establishing a foundation of trust for subsequent communication.

After understanding the parents’ concerns, physicians should advocate for and organize a multidisciplinary family meeting, integrating resources from ethics, social services, adolescent medicine, and other relevant specialties to help address parental doubts and provide professional guidance.

Based on the principle of minors’ right to be informed and relevant laws and regulations, physicians should, according to the patient’s maturity and individual circumstances, communicate directly with the patient about treatment options in a timely manner to ensure her right to information and participation.

Physicians should respect the family role and parental guardianship while safeguarding the best interests of the minor patient, rationally balancing the conflict between parental wishes and the patient’s future quality of life.

Physicians should advocate for and support the patient in obtaining necessary psychological, social, and peer support to promote her active involvement in decision-making regarding cancer treatment and fertility preservation, thereby optimizing her quality of life.

ethical_issues.question: In the context of Evelyn’s case, how should physicians ethically assess and address her cognitive and decision-making capacity regarding oncofertility options, given her status as a mature minor and potential disagreement with her parents?
keypoints: Physicians should assess the functional decision-making capacity of minor patients through effective communication, focusing on their ability to understand and articulate medical information, rather than relying solely on chronological age as the criterion.

Developmental psychology research indicates that children aged 11 to 13 and older typically possess the cognitive development necessary to comprehend information related to medical interventions, providing a reference point for evaluating their decision-making capacity.

Legally, informed consent requires that an individual voluntarily grants permission and possesses the ability to understand the nature, risks, benefits, and alternatives of the medical intervention, as well as the capacity to restate and explain the relevant information.
When minors have sufficient understanding of medical procedures but lack full capacity to exercise informed consent, they may be granted the right to assent or dissent to certain medical decisions.

Even if minors are deemed not fully competent to make medical decisions, they still retain the right to be informed; physicians should respect and fulfill their informational needs and promote their participation in the medical decision-making process.

title: Whom Should We Regard as Responsible for Health Record Inaccuracies That Hinder Population-Based Fact Finding?
case:
T is a 43-year-old woman who has well-controlled asthma and visits Dr A for an annual checkup from a rural part of the state. Dr A reviews T’s electronic health record (EHR), noting no documentation of COVID-19 vaccination. Dr A remembers results from a national study that rurality was associated with decreased odds for vaccination and asks T about her reasons for not getting any shots. “I did get 2 shots last year, but I didn’t get them here. That’s probably why you’re not seeing them. I plan to get a booster as soon it’s available.”
Dr A wonders why many other patients’ EHRs contain incomplete or inaccurate information and why. “Not only does this affect how I plan my time with my patients, but poor-quality data hinders epidemiological surveillance and tracking of population-level vaccine uptake. Wasn’t the harrowing transition we’ve all just made to EHRs supposed to eliminate problems like this?”
case_rewrite:
T, a 43-year-old woman with well-controlled asthma, arrives from her rural hometown for her annual checkup with Dr A. As Dr A reviews T’s electronic health record (EHR), she notices there is no record of any COVID-19 vaccinations. Recalling a recent national study linking rural residence to lower vaccination rates, Dr A decides to ask T about her vaccination status. 

T responds, “I did get two shots last year, but not at this clinic. That’s probably why you don’t see them in the record. I’m planning to get a booster as soon as it’s available.” 

Dr A reflects on the situation, noting that T’s EHR, like those of many other patients, contains gaps and inaccuracies. She wonders about the broader implications: “Incomplete or incorrect data not only affects my ability to provide the best care, but also undermines efforts to track vaccination rates and monitor public health. Wasn’t the challenging transition to EHRs supposed to solve problems like this?”

ethical_issues.question: How can the need for high-quality population health data be met while ensuring the protection of patient privacy and autonomy?
keypoints: Ensuring the simultaneous protection of patient privacy and autonomy while meeting the demand for high-quality population health data requires shared responsibility among healthcare systems, vendors, clinicians, patients, and researchers utilizing the data.
Research regulatory bodies provide foundational protections for patients by minimizing privacy risks, ensuring data security, and upholding investigator integrity.
Deidentification of integrated system-wide EHR data in accordance with privacy regulations is standard practice when used for research purposes.
Reconceptualizing patients as partners in data generation can motivate their engagement in initiatives aimed at collecting high-quality health data.
Opt-out mechanisms may partially restore patient agency over the use of their EHR data in research but have the potential to introduce bias into the data.
Data scientists and researchers bear the responsibility to ensure that high-quality EHR data are appropriately managed in population-level analyses and to safeguard against data breaches.

ethical_issues.question: In the context of inaccuracies in EHR data, how should responsibility for maintaining data accuracy be appropriately allocated?
keypoints: The responsibility for ensuring the accuracy of EHR data is shared among healthcare systems, vendors, clinicians, patients, and researchers who utilize the data.
Vendors and healthcare systems are accountable for the functionality, usability, and accuracy of the EHR.
Clinicians should actively engage with healthcare systems to ensure that data entry supports both clinical documentation and research purposes.
Patients should be regarded as partners in data generation to motivate their participation in high-quality data collection.
Data scientists and researchers must ensure that EHR data are appropriately managed in analyses and remain attentive to the five domains of data quality.

ethical_issues.question: How can the ethical issues between de-identification of EHR data and obtaining informed patient consent be balanced in research use?
keypoints: Deidentification is a standard practice in the use of EHR data for research purposes to protect patient privacy.
The use of EHR data without obtaining patient informed consent may result in patients being unaware of third-party uses of their data.
Opt-out mechanisms can partially restore patient autonomy over the use of their EHR data in research but may introduce bias into the data.
Classifying research based on deidentified EHR data as non–human subjects research may weaken regulatory oversight, thereby affecting population-level studies and inferences.

title: Surgery for Placebo Effect? Commentary 1
case:
Dr. Janus is an orthopedic surgeon who specializes in arthroscopic surgery and keeps current with medical research in his area. His goal is not only to provide the best care but also the most effective treatment at the lowest cost for his patients. Lately, he has treated many elderly patients with osteoarthritis of the knee and has noticed that this type of surgery is often a financial burden for his patients. Although the surgery is quite expensive, at $4,500 per knee, patients continue to request it, hoping to improve their mobility. In reviewing the current literature, Dr. Janus learns that the type of arthroscopic knee surgery he has been performing routinely (arthroscopic lavage and debridement) was shown in two different studies to provide no benefit over placebo; in fact, a strong placebo effect has been suggested. Dr. Janus feels that the patients he operated upon usually did improve, but he cannot rule out the placebo effect.
Mr. Marcus is a 70-year-old retiree living on his limited pension. He suffers from advanced osteoarthritis in his knees and would do anything to be able to walk more easily on his own. He says to Dr. Janus, “My best friend had this surgery last month, and he’s already getting around the golf course great. He says this surgery was the best thing he ever did for himself. I sure would love to be able to keep up with him now. What do you say, Doc, can you do this surgery for me?”
case_rewrite:
Dr. Janus, an orthopedic surgeon renowned for his expertise in arthroscopic procedures, continuously updates his practice in line with the latest medical research. He takes pride in not only delivering optimal patient care but also in finding the most effective and affordable treatments for those he serves. Recently, Dr. Janus has seen an increasing number of elderly patients with knee osteoarthritis, and he’s become increasingly concerned about the financial impact of surgery on them. Although the cost of arthroscopic knee surgery stands at $4,500 per knee—a considerable sum—patients remain eager to pursue it, hoping it will restore their mobility and independence.

During a recent review of emerging clinical literature, Dr. Janus discovered that the arthroscopic knee surgeries he has routinely performed—lavage and debridement—were found in two separate studies to offer no real advantage over placebo. The research strongly suggests that any improvement patients experience is likely attributable to the placebo effect rather than the procedure itself. While Dr. Janus has observed that many of his patients report feeling better after surgery, he cannot ignore the mounting evidence that questions the true efficacy of the intervention.

One morning, Dr. Janus meets Mr. Marcus, a 70-year-old retired man living on a modest pension. Mr. Marcus suffers from severe osteoarthritis in both knees and dreams of regaining the ability to walk without pain. Eager and hopeful, Mr. Marcus tells Dr. Janus, “My best friend had this surgery last month, and now he’s already back to walking the golf course. He says it was the best decision he ever made. I’d love to be able to keep up with him again. What do you think, Doc? Can you do this surgery for me?”

ethical_issues.question: How should physicians balance respecting patient autonomy with providing the best therapeutic recommendations when patients request surgery?
keypoints: Respect patient autonomy by acknowledging the patient’s right to participate in treatment decisions, while the physician must rationally weigh the potential benefits and risks of surgery.

Physicians should objectively evaluate the efficacy and necessity of arthroscopic knee surgery based on the most current clinical evidence, exercising particular caution when the therapeutic benefit is uncertain.

Physicians have a responsibility to communicate effectively, providing patients with a comprehensive explanation of the true effects of the surgery, potential risks, and the placebo effect, thereby facilitating a fully informed decision.
In cases where the likelihood of surgical success is low and adverse outcomes are possible, physicians should recommend that patients prioritize conservative treatment options.

Physicians must demonstrate empathy and patience, thoroughly explaining the scientific rationale behind treatment recommendations to promote patient understanding and respect for the physician’s professional judgment.

ethical_issues.question: In the context of limited medical resources, how should physicians make reasonable decisions balancing the needs of individual patients with the allocation of societal resources?
keypoints: Physicians must balance the interests of individual patients with the broader allocation of healthcare resources in clinical decision-making, posing an ethical challenge to the traditional role of the physician as patient advocate.
Physicians have no obligation to provide treatments of uncertain efficacy or lacking evidence-based support and should avoid unnecessary medical interventions.
Physicians are ethically and professionally responsible for the prudent use of limited resources, thereby minimizing waste.

The physician’s primary responsibility is to the patient before them, requiring an evidence-based assessment of the potential benefits and risks of surgery, while respecting the patient’s informed consent and values.

Physicians must navigate the tension between patient expectations and evidence-based medicine through effective communication and management of patient hopes, thereby facilitating rational decision-making.

ethical_issues.question: In light of the known possibility that the surgery may be ineffective or even harmful, how should the physician communicate the recommendation to refuse surgery to the patient and ensure that the patient understands and accepts this decision?
keypoints: Physicians should respect patient autonomy while rationally declining to provide surgical interventions that lack evidence of efficacy and may cause harm, based on the ethical principles of beneficence and nonmaleficence.

Physicians are obligated to clearly and sincerely communicate the latest evidence-based medical findings regarding arthroscopic debridement of the knee, emphasizing its lack of demonstrable benefit and potential risks, thereby assisting patients in developing a realistic understanding of the procedure’s limitations.

Physicians should recommend and thoroughly explain a comprehensive nonsurgical treatment regimen as the preferred initial approach, ensuring that patients fully comprehend and accept this conservative management pathway to facilitate informed decision-making aligned with their best interests.

title: Informed Consent: When and Why
case:
Dr. Wood opened the door to the exam room and smiled at Mrs. Robertson who was waiting in her patient gown. "Hi, Mrs. R, I'd like to ask a favor. Del, here, is on a family medicine rotation and, if it's OK with you, he'll assist with your exam today. Is that alright with you?"
"Sure," replied Mrs. Robertson, "I'm all for medical education."
"Thank you," said Del.
"Why don't you begin by checking Mrs. Robertson's vitals," instructed Dr. Wood. After noting these results, Dr. Wood took over the physical, asking questions and sharing a few observations with Del. Dr. Wood then asked Mrs. Robertson to undo her gown and he proceeded to conduct a breast exam by raising her arm, pressing on her breast to check for lumps, and looking carefully at the nipples for signs of discharge. After examining both breasts and palpating Mrs. Robertson's liver and ovaries, Dr. Wood asked Del to step outside and send the nurse in, so he could collect a Pap smear.
After Mrs. Robertson left, Del said, "This is my first rotation, and one thing I've been wondering, Dr. Wood, is do we just do all of the standard aspects of the physical on our patients without asking? Like the breast exam, for instance. Because I'm so young and everything, I feel as though I should ask permission, but you just went right ahead. She didn't object so I guess it's OK, but is that routine? Where is the line where I have to ask or get consent? Did you ask consent to do the Pap, or is it all assumed consent?
Dr. Wood considered Del 's question. "That's a really good point. Mrs. Roberston has been my patient for a while, for one thing. But still, I don't recall ever having my professors tell me specifically what are or are not the trigger events for a consent--verbal or otherwise--in a routine check-up. I can't remember a lot of articles on that, and I've been in practice for quite a while."
case_rewrite:
Dr. Wood stepped into the exam room, greeting Mrs. Robertson with a warm smile as she sat waiting in her patient gown. “Hello, Mrs. Robertson. I’d like to ask you for a favor. Del, here, is a medical student currently completing a family medicine rotation with me. If you’re comfortable with it, he’ll be assisting with your exam today. Would that be alright?”

Mrs. Robertson nodded, a friendly expression on her face. “Of course, Dr. Wood. I’m happy to help with training.”

“Thank you so much,” Del replied appreciatively.

Dr. Wood turned to Del. “Why don’t you start by checking Mrs. Robertson’s vital signs?” Del moved forward, carefully measuring and recording her blood pressure, pulse, and temperature. When he finished, Dr. Wood reviewed the numbers and continued with the rest of the physical, engaging Del by asking questions and pointing out clinical findings as he went.

Soon, Dr. Wood addressed Mrs. Robertson. “I’ll need to examine your breasts now. Please undo your gown in the front and raise your arm for me.” Mrs. Robertson complied, and Dr. Wood methodically palpated each breast, checking for any masses, and inspected the nipples for signs of discharge. He then continued to examine her abdomen, palpating her liver and ovaries.

Once the initial examination was complete, Dr. Wood turned to Del. “Could you step outside and ask the nurse to come in, please?” Del nodded, stepping out as Dr. Wood prepared to collect a Pap smear with the nurse present.

Later, after Mrs. Robertson had left the office, Del lingered in the hallway, a question on his mind. “Dr. Wood, this is my first clinical rotation, and I’ve been thinking—during a standard exam, like today’s, do we need to ask for specific permission before doing things like a breast exam? I’m younger and not as experienced, so it feels like I should get explicit consent, but you just went ahead with it. She didn’t seem uncomfortable, but is that the norm? How do we know when to ask for verbal or written consent, especially for things like a Pap smear? Is it assumed, or do we need to be more formal about it?”

Dr. Wood paused, reflecting on the question. “That’s a very important consideration, Del. I’ve known Mrs. Robertson for a long time, and we have a good rapport, but now that you mention it, I can’t recall any specific guidelines from my training about which parts of the physical require explicit consent, verbal or otherwise, during routine check-ups. There’s not a lot of literature on the topic either, even though I’ve been practicing for many years.”

ethical_issues.question: In a routine physical examination, which components require explicit informed consent from the patient?
keypoints: Patients should be informed of and consent to all proposed aspects of care, especially examinations and procedures involving sensitive areas or topics.

When an intervention entails increased risk, involves highly sensitive privacy concerns, or may significantly impact the patient’s life, additional physician-patient discussion is required to obtain explicit consent.

Patients must be informed about the involvement of medical students or other trainees in their care and should be allowed to grant or withhold specific authorization for sensitive history-taking and examination components.

Patients have the right to know the qualifications and experience level of the individual performing the procedure; physicians should address and manage any particular concerns or anxieties the patient may have during the consent discussion.

In cases of uncertainty regarding the need for explicit consent, priority should be given to communicating with the patient through informal discussion explaining the necessity of the intervention and obtaining verbal assent, which is generally considered sufficient consent.

ethical_issues.question: During the involvement of medical students in the diagnostic and treatment process, how should physicians inform patients and obtain their consent in order to respect the patients’ autonomy and privacy needs?
keypoints: Physicians should inform patients about the involvement of medical students in their care, introduce all personnel present along with their roles, and obtain the patient’s consent in order to respect the patient’s autonomy.
Patients have the right to know the qualifications and experience levels of all individuals participating in their care, and physicians should provide truthful disclosure regarding this information.

For invasive or sensitive examinations (such as breast exams), physicians must obtain explicit authorization from the patient and allow the patient to refuse, thereby protecting the patient’s privacy and autonomy.
When discussing highly personal or sensitive matters, physicians should respect the boundaries set by the patient, proactively communicate, and accommodate the patient’s privacy needs.

ethical_issues.question: How should physicians ensure that informed consent is fully respected and confirmed when conducting examinations involving sensitive areas of the body?
keypoints: When conducting examinations involving sensitive areas of the body, the physician should clearly explain to the patient, through an informal discussion, the necessity, content, and procedure of the examination, and obtain the patient’s verbal consent after this explanation.

The physician should explicitly inform the patient of the identities and roles of all individuals present, especially regarding the participation of medical students, and should respect the patient’s right to authorize or refuse such participation.
The physician should respect the patient’s need for privacy, ensuring that the examination environment and process protect the patient’s personal privacy and dignity.

The physician should be attentive to the patient’s feelings, provide opportunities for the patient to ask questions and express preferences, and thereby demonstrate respect for the patient’s autonomy.

For examinations that are higher risk or may significantly affect the patient’s life, more thorough communication should be conducted, and, when necessary, additional physician-patient discussions should be held to confirm informed consent.

title: Can Parents of a Child with Autism Refuse Treatment for Him?
case:
Dr. Pittman was nearing the end of her shift at a busy community clinic on a Friday afternoon. Her last appointment was with a new patient, Dayton, a 6-year-old boy who, according to his parents, had an earache. Dr. Pittman quickly diagnosed otitis media and talked with Dayton’s parents about treatment.
Dayton’s behavior troubled Dr. Pittman far more than his inflamed eardrum. He did not make eye contact or respond to her questions. He flinched whenever she approached him, cried out in fear when she peered in his ear with an otoscope, and hopped up and down, shaking his hands compulsively, several times during the visit. When Dr. Pittman questioned Dayton’s parents about his behavior, they told her he had been diagnosed with autism at age 4. His development, they said, was delayed.
She asked what treatment Dayton’s parents had sought for him, and the answer shocked her. They were members of the autism self-advocacy movement and believed that Dayton’s condition was simply an example of neurodiversity and was not pathologic. They clearly adored their son, doting on him during the clinic visit and telling Dr. Pittman how they home-schooled him after the public school system failed to meet his social and educational needs. They accepted Dayton as he was and were determined to provide him with lifetime care.
Dr. Pittman viewed Dayton’s situation differently. She knew that with proper therapy and medication his condition could improve considerably—but only if treatment were begun as soon as possible. She worked at a nearby autism clinic, where Dayton could probably qualify for long-term treatment. When she mentioned this to Dayton’s parents, they wanted nothing to do with it. They were adamant in their belief that Dayton’s condition required no medical intervention.
Dr. Pittman had encountered many adult patients with culture-based opinions about their health problems that she found hard to understand, but this was the first time she’d disagreed so fundamentally with parents about a situation that she believed would harm their child by limiting his future opportunities. She fought the urge to reprimand them for what she considered their neglect of his debilitating developmental problem. Did their treatment constitute child endangerment, she wondered? Would she be justified in contacting a child protection agency?
case_rewrite:
Dr. Pittman was wrapping up her hectic Friday shift at the bustling community clinic when her last patient of the day arrived: Dayton, a 6-year-old boy brought in by his parents for an earache. After a brief evaluation, Dr. Pittman quickly diagnosed Dayton with otitis media and began discussing treatment options with his parents.

Yet, it wasn’t Dayton’s ear that most concerned Dr. Pittman. Throughout the appointment, Dayton avoided eye contact and didn’t respond to her questions. Each time Dr. Pittman approached, he recoiled noticeably, and when she examined his ear with the otoscope, he cried out in fear. Periodically, Dayton would hop in place, shaking his hands in a repetitive, compulsive way. Troubled by these behaviors, Dr. Pittman gently inquired about Dayton’s development.

Dayton’s parents explained that he had been diagnosed with autism at the age of four and that his development was delayed. When Dr. Pittman asked what kind of support or therapies they had pursued, the parents’ response surprised her. They explained that they were strong advocates of the autism self-advocacy movement, viewing Dayton’s condition as an expression of neurodiversity rather than a disorder. Throughout the visit, their affection for Dayton was clear; they attended to him with patience and warmth, sharing that they had chosen to homeschool him after finding the public school system unable to accommodate his social and educational needs. They spoke with conviction about accepting Dayton as he was and providing for his needs themselves, both now and in the future.

Dr. Pittman, however, saw things differently. She knew from her experience at a local autism clinic that early intervention—including therapy and, in some cases, medication—could make a significant difference in outcomes for a child like Dayton, especially if begun promptly. She mentioned the clinic to Dayton’s parents, suggesting that he might benefit from long-term treatment services. But the parents firmly rejected the idea, insisting that Dayton did not require medical intervention, and reiterating their commitment to supporting him outside of medical systems.

Dr. Pittman had often encountered adult patients whose cultural perspectives on health differed from her own, but she had never faced such a fundamental disagreement with parents over a course of action that, in her view, could limit a child’s future possibilities. She struggled to suppress her urge to chastise them for what she saw as neglecting a serious developmental issue. Was this parental refusal putting Dayton at risk? Did it cross the line into child endangerment? Dr. Pittman found herself wondering whether she would be justified in contacting child protective services.

ethical_issues.question: Do parents have the right to refuse medical intervention for a child with autism, especially when such refusal may impact the child’s future well-being?
keypoints: Parents are generally regarded as the primary decision-makers for their children because they possess the most comprehensive understanding of the child’s fundamental interests and bear caregiving responsibilities; however, respect for parental choices is not absolute and must be balanced with the child’s interests and developing autonomy.

When parental decisions may expose the child to imminent and serious health risks or irreversible harm, the principles of beneficence and nonmaleficence should take precedence over respect for parental autonomy, and physicians have a duty to intervene to protect the child’s welfare.

If a treatment is proven effective with manageable risks and no suitable alternatives exist, physicians have a justified basis to override parental refusal of treatment, provided that the severity and imminence of harm are carefully assessed.

Early intervention for autism heavily depends on active family involvement; coercive treatment approaches may provoke resistance, diminish therapeutic efficacy, and potentially lead to complete disengagement from medical care by the family.

Therefore, establishing a trusting and collaborative therapeutic alliance that respects family values and incrementally promotes the child’s well-being represents a more pragmatic and effective strategy.

ethical_issues.question: How can a balance be achieved between respecting parental autonomy and promoting the well-being of the child, especially in situations where treatment requires active parental involvement?
keypoints: Physicians should understand and respect the parents’ cultural values and perspectives regarding autism, avoid simplistic pathologization, and promote individualized care plans that incorporate diverse viewpoints.

Coercive treatment may exacerbate conflict between the physician and the family, reduce parental willingness to cooperate, and ultimately undermine Dayton’s long-term well-being; therefore, priority should be given to building trust and a therapeutic alliance to foster collaboration.

The therapeutic alliance is grounded in the shared commitment of the physician and parents to serve Dayton’s best interests, and is developed through ongoing communication and negotiation to identify mutually acceptable treatment options.

ethical_issues.question: Under what circumstances may a physician override parental refusal of treatment in order to ensure that a child is not placed at significant and imminent risk of harm?
keypoints: When parental decisions place the child at significant and imminent risk of serious harm, the physician should intervene immediately.
The refused intervention must be proven effective, low-risk, capable of preventing harm, and its anticipated benefits must clearly outweigh those of the option chosen by the parents.
If refusal of treatment is likely to cause serious and irreversible harm to the child’s current health or future autonomy, the physician is justified in intervening.
When there is no alternative that infringes less on parental autonomy and is more acceptable to the parents, the physician may override the parents’ decision.
State intervention is reasonable in similar circumstances, and such intervention would generally be accepted by most rational parents.

title: Frame Feedback to Improve Professional Performance: Colleague-to-Colleague Communication.
case: You are a radiologist in a hospital-based group practice. While one of your partners is on vacation, an internist calls you because your partner read a head MRI for one of his patients a short while ago. The internist is concerned because his patient's headaches are becoming worse, and she is now developing visual field deficits. Before considering a second MRI, the internist would like you to review the first one. When you get off the phone, you pull the patient's (Mrs. Kirk's) MRI; there is a suspicious "spot" in the pituitary gland that you think called for further evaluation. This difference of opinion has happened twice before with radiographic studies that were originally read by your partner and reviewed by you over the last 6 months. You think he is making some obvious omissions in his readings, and you recognize that something must be done. Patient safety could be at risk. You decide that the best course of action is to talk to your partner as soon as he returns from vacation. What do you say to him and how do you say it?
case_rewrite:
You are a radiologist working in a hospital-based group practice. While your colleague Dr. W is on vacation, you receive a call from SB, an internist, regarding a mutual patient. SB explains that his patient, Mrs. Kirk, has been experiencing worsening headaches and has now developed visual field deficits. SB mentions that Dr. W recently read Mrs. Kirk’s head MRI, but before ordering another scan, he asks if you could review the initial images.

After finishing the call, you retrieve Mrs. Kirk’s MRI and examine it closely. You notice a suspicious area in the pituitary gland that you believe deserved further evaluation—something you do not see mentioned in Dr. W’s original report. This is not the first occurrence: over the past six months, you have twice before disagreed with Dr. W’s interpretations after reviewing studies he initially read. In both previous cases, you felt that Dr. W overlooked findings that seemed apparent and important to you.

These repeated discrepancies raise serious concerns for you about Dr. W’s pattern of omissions in his radiology readings. Recognizing that patient safety might be at risk, you feel an ethical obligation to address the issue. After careful consideration, you resolve that the most appropriate course is to speak directly with Dr. W as soon as he returns from vacation.

You plan to arrange a private, undisturbed meeting with Dr. W. In this conversation, you intend to present your concerns using clear, factual examples, focusing specifically on the differences you have observed in the readings, including Mrs. Kirk’s case and the two earlier incidents. Your goal is to express your worries for both patient safety and Dr. W’s professional development, and to approach the issue collaboratively, inviting Dr. W to share his perspective and participate in identifying the underlying causes. You hope to work together in finding constructive solutions and ensuring that future patient care is not compromised.

ethical_issues.question: How can critical feedback be provided to a colleague in a constructive manner while ensuring patient safety?
keypoints: When recognizing that a pattern of potential errors has emerged and may jeopardize patient safety, physicians have an ethical obligation to take timely action to ensure the quality of diagnosis and treatment as well as patient safety.
Choose an undisturbed and private setting for delivering feedback to avoid triggering defensiveness and to foster an open and respectful communication environment.
Provide feedback using descriptive, specific, and fact-based data, focusing on key issues while avoiding vague or evaluative language.

Clearly articulate dual concerns for both patient safety and the professional development of the colleague, thereby promoting constructive collaboration and a shared willingness to resolve the problem.
