Allow the colleague ample opportunity to respond, encouraging their engagement in problem analysis and the formulation of improvement plans; feedback should focus on remediable behaviors and directions for improvement.

ethical_issues.question: How can one communicate effectively about a colleague’s clinical errors without triggering a defensive response?
keypoints: Frame the communication as a collaborative problem-solving process between allies, emphasizing cooperation rather than blame.
Choose an undisturbed, private setting and arrange a planned face-to-face meeting, avoiding surprise or impromptu interactions.
Use descriptive, specific data and facts, focusing on key points for improvement while avoiding vague or evaluative language.
Clearly express dual concerns for patient safety and the colleague’s professional development to foster mutual understanding and support.
Provide the colleague with ample opportunity to respond, encouraging their active participation in problem analysis and solution formulation.
Ensure feedback targets remediable behaviors and decisions, aiming to optimize clinical outcomes and professional performance.

ethical_issues.question: In the context of a colleague’s ongoing clinical errors, how should one balance ethical responsibilities with professional relationships and take appropriate actions to prevent further risk to patients?
keypoints: Physicians have an ethical obligation to ensure the accuracy of diagnosis and treatment, and must take timely action to safeguard patient safety upon identifying clinical errors made by colleagues.
Communication with colleagues should be conducted in a constructive and collaborative manner, avoiding the provocation of defensiveness and thereby promoting professional improvement.
Feedback should be delivered in a private, undisturbed setting, employing specific, descriptive language grounded in factual data, and focusing on key issues.

It is essential to thoroughly understand the underlying causes of errors, paying attention to potential factors such as incompetence, mental health concerns, or other influences, and to recommend that colleagues seek professional support when necessary.
Colleagues should be given ample opportunity to respond, encouraging their active participation in problem analysis and resolution; feedback should concentrate on specific, remediable behaviors.
If clinical errors persist despite communication efforts, reporting to a quality assurance committee should be considered, but this measure ought to be reserved as a last resort.

title: Interprofessional Training: Not Optional in Good Medical Education
case:
As director of an obstetrics and gynecology rotation, one of Dr. Chan’s goals is to emphasize interprofessional collaboration so that her students will be prepared for the cross-disciplinary practice environments into which they will be graduating. In particular, she wants students to learn from nurse midwives, whom she admires as experts in normal deliveries, as role models and leaders in patient-centered care, and as fellow professionals who offer safe labor and delivery options for women with low-risk pregnancies. To achieve these goals, Dr. Chan divides each student’s time on this rotation into two parts, one with an ob-gyn physician and one with a nurse midwife.
After the student assignments have been distributed, Dr. Chan receives an email from a student that says, “I recognize that many women want midwives for their deliveries, but I came to medical school to learn what physicians do. I will soon have to make a decision about which residency to do, and I want as much time as possible to work with physicians. I would like my schedule to be changed so I can spend my time in medical school learning from physicians, not nurses or midwives.”
Dr. Chan has received a few emails like this in the past. She is concerned that accommodating this student’s request will send a message to other students that learning what nurse midwives do is optional and unimportant. How ought Dr. Chan to address students with the kinds of concerns raised in the email?
case_rewrite:
As the director of the obstetrics and gynecology rotation, Dr. Chan is deeply committed to preparing her students for the realities of modern, team-based clinical practice. She believes that interprofessional collaboration is essential, and she particularly values the expertise and patient-centered approach of nurse midwives. Dr. Chan views nurse midwives not only as experts in normal deliveries but also as exemplary leaders in providing holistic care and as essential partners in ensuring safe outcomes for women with low-risk pregnancies. With these principles in mind, Dr. Chan has structured the rotation so that each student spends half of their time working alongside an ob-gyn physician and the other half learning from a certified nurse midwife.

After distributing the assignment schedules, Dr. Chan receives an email from a student named SB. The message reads, “I understand that many women prefer midwives for their deliveries, but my main reason for coming to medical school was to learn what physicians do. Since I’ll soon need to decide on a residency, I want to maximize my exposure to physician practice. I would like my schedule adjusted so that I spend my time learning from physicians, not from nurses or midwives.”

This is not the first time Dr. Chan has received such a request. She is concerned that granting SB’s wish would signal to the rest of the students that learning from nurse midwives is optional or less important, undermining the goals of the rotation and the model of collaborative care she seeks to promote. Dr. Chan now faces the task of responding to SB’s concerns in a way that upholds the educational standards of the program and reinforces the significance of interprofessional learning.

ethical_issues.question: Should medical students have the option, during interprofessional training, to choose to learn only from physicians and not from nurse midwives?
keypoints: Medical students do not have the right to opt out of interprofessional training; interprofessional team collaboration is fundamental to contemporary medical practice, and students must develop collaborative skills and an understanding of multidisciplinary roles.

Certified nurse midwives are highly specialized health professionals; by learning from midwives in the management of normal pregnancy and childbirth, medical students receive an ideal introduction to obstetrics and gynecology that aligns with the educational progression from the normal to the pathological.

Receiving training exclusively from physicians leads to a lack of understanding of the skills and terminology of other key health professionals, impairs comprehension of multidisciplinary team collaboration, and hinders effective cooperation within future healthcare teams.

Dr. Chan should refuse the student’s request to work only with physicians, fulfill her educational responsibility to correct the student’s misconceptions about medicine as a whole and the physician’s role specifically, and emphasize the importance of interprofessional training in cultivating competent practitioners and adapting to the modern healthcare environment.

ethical_issues.question: Should medical education emphasize interprofessional collaboration to prepare students for future team-based practice environments?
keypoints: Medical education must emphasize interprofessional collaboration, enabling students to learn within clinical teams and preparing them to adapt to team-based medical practice after graduation.

Interprofessional education helps students understand the roles and value of each professional team member, fosters mutual respect and effective communication, and thereby enhances the comprehensiveness and quality of patient care.

Students who are trained solely from the physician’s perspective may struggle to function effectively in future multidisciplinary teams and may have significant gaps in their understanding of the skills and terminology of other health professionals.

The current healthcare environment prioritizes multidisciplinary collaboration, and integrating the expertise of non-physician clinicians is a key strategy for meeting the complex needs of patients and achieving high-quality care.

Clinical clerkships should authentically reflect the operational model of modern healthcare teams; educational arrangements that separate students from shared interprofessional responsibility for patients lack justification and practical relevance.

ethical_issues.question: Does the medical school have a responsibility to correct students’ misconceptions about the nature of medicine as a whole and the role of physicians, particularly in the context of interprofessional collaboration?
keypoints: Medical schools have a responsibility to correct students’ misconceptions about the nature of medicine as a whole and the role of physicians, particularly in the context of interprofessional collaboration, ensuring that students understand the contemporary meaning of the physician’s role.

Interprofessional collaboration reflects the team-based nature and practice model of modern medicine; medical schools should introduce this model to cultivate students’ ability and attitude to learn from all members of the health care team.

Medical students must participate in interprofessional team-based care in order to understand and respect the professional value of each team member, thereby becoming competent for the complex collaborative clinical environments of the future.

tags: Disparities in health and health care/Social determinants
title: How Should Clinicians Respond if Patient HIV Denial Could Exacerbate Racial Health Inequities?
case:
M is a 56-year-old Black man with human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) who is hospitalized with failure to thrive and dyspnea. Prior to hospitalization, M did not have an infectious disease or a primary care physician, and he was not taking antiretroviral therapy (ART). In the past 20 years, when physicians have tried to speak to M about his HIV diagnosis and benefits of ART, he has said, “I don’t have HIV,” or “I don’t talk about that.”
M has a CD4+ count of 3 cells/μL. He is diagnosed withPneumocystis jiroveciipneumonia,norovirus diarrhea, and acquired immune deficiency syndrome (AIDS) wasting syndrome. During hospitalization, his relationships with clinicians worsen; if a clinician mentions HIV, M breaks eye contact, becomes silent, and interacts minimally.
An infectious disease specialist, Dr D, visits M to talk about his pneumonia. M consents to antibiotics to treat this infection and appears interested in strengthening his immune system “in other ways,” as Dr D suggests. Dr D continues to use M’s positive response as an opportunity to motivate M to take ART and is tempted to give M ART without saying it is HIV treatment. She is worried about deceiving M, taking unfair advantage of his lower health literacy, and upsetting him if he discovers that “immunity booster pills” are actually ART. Since low engagement with health care and worse health outcomes are prevalent in some communities of color and sometimes accompanied by misinformation about HIV, Dr D is encouraged by M’s engagement. She considers whether it might be best to get him to take ART, even unknowingly, to save his life.
case_rewrite:
M is a 56-year-old Black man living with human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) who has been admitted to the hospital for failure to thrive and shortness of breath. Before this admission, M did not have a relationship with an infectious disease specialist or a primary care provider, and he was not on antiretroviral therapy (ART). Over the past two decades, whenever physicians attempted to discuss his HIV diagnosis or the potential benefits of ART, M would respond by saying, “I don’t have HIV,” or, “I don’t talk about that.”

On admission, M is found to have a CD4+ count of 3 cells/μL. He is diagnosed with Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia, norovirus-induced diarrhea, and AIDS wasting syndrome. During his stay, M’s interactions with the clinical team become increasingly strained; whenever a clinician mentions HIV, M averts his gaze, becomes withdrawn, and minimizes communication.

Dr D, an infectious disease specialist, visits M to discuss his pneumonia. M agrees to receive antibiotics for the infection and expresses some interest when Dr D talks about ways to strengthen his immune system. Dr D sees this as an encouraging sign and tries to use this opening to motivate M to consider ART. However, M’s longstanding denial and discomfort around his diagnosis present significant barriers. Dr D finds herself tempted to provide M with ART without explicitly stating that it is treatment for HIV—perhaps referring to the medication as “immunity boosters.” She worries about deceiving M, especially given his limited health literacy, and fears the consequences if M discovers that these “immunity boosters” are actually ART. Dr D is also mindful that mistrust of the healthcare system, low engagement, and misinformation about HIV are common in some communities of color, which can further complicate care and outcomes.

Although encouraged by M’s willingness to engage on some level, Dr D wrestles with the ethical dilemma: whether administering ART without M’s explicit, informed consent—by withholding the true nature of the medication—could ever be justified in an effort to save his life.

ethical_issues.question: In the case of M, who persistently denies his HIV diagnosis, is it ethically permissible for clinicians to initiate antiretroviral therapy (ART) without his explicit informed consent in order to preserve his life?
keypoints: Clinicians should not administer antiretroviral therapy (ART) without informing the patient, as this constitutes deception and violates medical ethical and legal standards.

Deceiving patients can severely undermine the trust between clinician and patient, leading to reduced or refused healthcare engagement in the future and negatively impacting long-term treatment outcomes.

Trust is central to the therapeutic alliance; clinicians must be transparent in all communications by honestly informing patients about the nature and purpose of the treatments provided, including ART.

ethical_issues.question: How should clinicians establish trust and form an effective therapeutic alliance when patients refuse to discuss their risk behaviors or infection status due to social or internalized stigma?
keypoints: Clinicians must recognize that social and internalized stigma underlie patients’ coping mechanisms such as denial, and understand how these factors contribute to communication barriers and treatment resistance.
Clinicians should build trust and facilitate open dialogue gradually through attentive listening, open-ended questioning, avoidance of judgmental communication, and information sharing.
Physicians ought to invest unstructured time to gain deeper insight into the patient, demonstrating sincere listening and a commitment to the patient’s best interests.
Clinicians need to comprehend the patient’s sources of meaning in life and greatest fears, collaboratively developing individualized care plans aligned with the patient’s goals and concerns.

The obligation to honesty supersedes the impulse to treat; physicians must not deceive patients in pursuit of therapeutic outcomes, but rather respect patients’ informed consent rights and uphold the integrity of the clinician-patient relationship.

title: Smoking and Medicaid Benefits
case:
Dr. Smith's spirits fell as soon as he noticed Jack fidgeting uncomfortably in the waiting area of his small private practice office. Jack usually dreaded even the thought of seeing a doctor.
Upon his first visit to Dr. Smith's office several years ago, Jack was diagnosed with diabetes and high blood pressure. As a result, Dr. Smith placed him on four different medications, one of which was insulin.
Whenever Jack came in for a checkup, Dr. Smith would repeat his pleas that Jack quit smoking and adopt healthier lifestyle choices. Jack's usual response to the former: "Why the hell should I stop smoking when I've done it for 20 years now?" His response to the latter: "Doc, I eat what I like and what I can afford. That's it. I'm not going to waste money on stuff I won't eat."
On this visit, Dr. Smith hoped things would be different. He reminded Jack, who received Medicaid benefits, that he had signed a contract with the state of West Virginia entitling him to additional health benefits—such as weight-loss and anti-smoking programs, mental health services, diabetes management classes, and cardiac rehabilitation—if he kept his medical appointments, took his medications, and followed health improvement plans. If he reneged on these obligations—and so far, Jack had—Dr. Smith would be forced to report this noncompliance to the state. Jack would still get basic Medicaid services but would only get four free drug prescriptions per month, among other limitations.
"So this is what happens when there's a crunch for taxpayer money," Dr. Smith thought to himself. "You end up doling out service based on compliance." What really worried Dr. Smith was that if Jack ever got sick with an infection, for example, one that required antibiotic treatment, and the prescription exceeded the dollar limit, there would be no telling what would happen to Jack or his kids.
case_rewrite:
Dr. Smith’s mood sank as he spotted Jack shifting restlessly in the waiting room of his modest private practice. Jack, never one for doctor visits, looked more uneasy than usual.

Years ago, during Jack’s first appointment, Dr. Smith had diagnosed him with diabetes and high blood pressure. Since then, Jack had been prescribed four medications, including insulin. At every follow-up, Dr. Smith would urge Jack to quit smoking and work on healthier habits. Jack’s answers were always the same: “Why should I quit after twenty years?” he would grumble about smoking. As for diet, Jack would shrug and say, “Doc, I eat what I like and what I can afford. I’m not spending money on stuff I won’t eat.”

Today, Dr. Smith hoped for a change. He reminded Jack, who was covered by Medicaid, of the contract he’d signed with the state of West Virginia. This agreement granted Jack access to extra health benefits—weight-loss and anti-smoking programs, mental health counseling, diabetes classes, even cardiac rehab—so long as he kept appointments, took his medications, and followed recommended health plans. If Jack failed to meet these terms—and so far, he had—Dr. Smith would be obligated to report his noncompliance to the state. Jack would still receive basic Medicaid, but with new restrictions: only four free prescriptions per month and other cutbacks.

“So this is what it comes to when public funds are tight,” Dr. Smith thought, troubled. “Care gets rationed according to compliance.” What truly unsettled him was the risk Jack faced if he developed an illness like an infection needing antibiotics. If Jack’s prescriptions exceeded the Medicaid limit, there was no telling what would happen to him—or to his children.

ethical_issues.question: Does the West Virginia Medicaid reform serve the best interests of patients, or does it threaten patient interests?
keypoints: The West Virginia Medicaid reform aims to promote improvement in unhealthy lifestyles and enhance health outcomes while reducing healthcare costs by strengthening personal health responsibility and establishing incentive mechanisms.
Proponents argue that the program motivates patients to engage in self-management and adhere to treatment plans, thereby improving health outcomes and conserving public healthcare resources.

Opponents contend that the program may undermine physician-patient trust, increase ethical conflicts for physicians between legal obligations and the duty to act in the patient’s best interests, and disrupt traditional models of the physician-patient relationship.

Physicians must distinguish between achievable and ideal patient behaviors, avoiding the simplistic classification of patients as noncompliant when goals are not fully met, and should adjust treatment and management plans through physician-patient communication.

The program may result in service limitations for economically disadvantaged patients, exacerbating health inequities; particular attention must be paid to the economic and health risks faced by patients and their families.

Only through fair pilot implementation, ongoing monitoring, and multi-stakeholder feedback can the reform be comprehensively evaluated to determine whether it truly serves the best interests of patients and safeguards their rights.

title: How Should MLP Clinicians and Attorneys Help Veterans Secure Disability Benefits When Health Records Documentation Is Insufficient?
case:
AB is 55 years old, unhoused, self-employed, divorced and has no children. He is unable to work due to a back injury sustained during his job as a contractor 2 years ago that limits his functional ability. He is currently living in his van and on alternating nights at a local shelter. A staff member at the shelter advises AB to file a claim for social security disability insurance (SSDI) benefits. AB tells the staffer he tried but found the application so complicated he gave up. The staff member then remembers that AB is a veteran and connects him to a new medical-legal partnership (MLP) housed on the grounds of the local Veterans Health Administration (VHA) medical center. At the VHA MLP, AB is fortunate to meet an attorney with considerable experience in a community MLP, JD, who arranges for AB to see Dr C, a new primary care physician in the VHA MLP. JD explains to Dr C that to file an SSDI claim, AB must be able to demonstrate that he has been unable to work for the last year due to his back injury.
Preparing for her first visit with AB, Dr C learns that AB has an existing VHA electronic health record (EHR): AB served in the army as a mechanic during Operation Iraqi Freedom. AB’s health record shows that, after being discharged from the military, he was seen at a VHA medical center in another city for symptoms of posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) and suicidal ideation. AB attended a few appointments but then moved across the country and never reestablished care in the VHA system or in the community. After examining AB, Dr C tells JD she has “no question that AB’s back injury would likely qualify for SSDI,” and she also thought his PTSD would meet diagnostic criteria, yet she does not believe there is currently adequate evidence to support either claim. She wants to help AB get the benefits he deserves but worries about compromising her professional integrity by engaging in questionable documentation practices. JD reassures her they both have the same goal—not to manipulate the health record but to get AB the care he needs and deserves. They meet to discuss their options and consider next steps.
case_rewrite:
AB, a 55-year-old veteran, is currently unhoused, self-employed, divorced, and has no children. Two years ago, while working as a contractor, AB suffered a debilitating back injury that now severely limits his ability to work. Without stable employment or income, he lives in his van and occasionally stays at a local shelter. One evening, a shelter staff member suggests that AB apply for Social Security Disability Insurance (SSDI) benefits. AB explains that he previously attempted to apply, but found the process so confusing and overwhelming that he ultimately gave up. The staff member, recalling AB’s veteran status, connects him with a recently established medical-legal partnership (MLP) located at the local Veterans Health Administration (VHA) medical center.

At the VHA MLP, AB meets JD, an attorney with significant experience working in community MLPs. JD quickly arranges for AB to have a medical evaluation with Dr C, a new primary care physician in the VHA MLP. JD informs Dr C that for AB’s SSDI application to be successful, AB will need clear documentation showing he has been unable to work for at least a year due to his back injury.

As Dr C prepares for her first appointment with AB, she accesses his VHA electronic health record (EHR). She learns that AB served as a mechanic in the army during Operation Iraqi Freedom, and that after his military discharge, he had been seen at a VHA medical center in another state for symptoms of posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) and suicidal ideation. The record shows that AB attended a handful of appointments but, after moving across the country, did not reestablish care, either within the VHA system or in the community.

During her assessment, Dr C finds that AB’s back injury appears serious enough to meet SSDI criteria, and she suspects that his PTSD would also fulfill diagnostic requirements. However, she notes that the available documentation is insufficient to substantiate either claim at present. Dr C expresses to JD her strong desire to help AB secure the benefits he deserves, but she is concerned about maintaining her professional integrity and does not want to engage in questionable documentation practices. JD reassures her that their shared objective is to ensure AB receives the care and support he is entitled to, not to manipulate medical records. Together, they begin to discuss their available options and consider next steps.

ethical_issues.question: In assisting AB with his application for disability benefits, how should clinicians and attorneys within the MLP balance their ethical obligations to provide accurate and thorough medical documentation with their duty to advocate for AB’s best interests, particularly when existing health records are insufficient?
keypoints: The distinctiveness of VHA-MLPs enables attorneys and clinicians to address the disability diagnostic and documentation dilemma through a shared commitment to mitigating the adverse effects of social determinants of health.
VHA practitioners can utilize comprehensive, longitudinal electronic health records to bridge documentation gaps, ensuring the provision of accurate and complete medical records.
Collaborating with VHA psychologists to conduct comprehensive assessments can establish a stronger evidentiary basis to support disability benefits claims.
Clinicians and attorneys should avoid an excessive focus on the instrumental goal of securing SSDI financial benefits to prevent the emergence of ethical conflicts.

MLPs can collaboratively facilitate access to a broader range of VHA services that, in the long term, enhance veterans’ overall well-being and align with the VHA’s strategic priority to reduce suicide rates among marginalized, underserved, and unhoused veterans.

ethical_issues.question: Within the VHA-MLP framework, how can attorneys and clinicians coordinate their respective ethical obligations to avoid conflicts in diagnostic documentation?
keypoints: Attorneys and clinicians should shift the foundation of their collaboration from administrative objectives to a shared commitment centered on patient well-being in order to avoid ethical conflicts.
The distinctiveness of VHA MLPs enables attorneys and clinicians to coordinate the disability diagnostic and documentation process by addressing the adverse impact of social determinants of health.

By leveraging the VHA’s comprehensive longitudinal electronic health record, clinicians can submit medically accurate documentation, which attorneys can translate into legal language to support claims.

The VHA has implemented measures to facilitate information sharing, including requiring veterans to sign authorizations for the release of health information and mandating clinicians to complete non-VA medical and social service forms.

ethical_issues.question: How can JD, the MLP attorney, and VHA clinicians ethically utilize AB’s comprehensive electronic health record to support his disability benefits claim while maintaining professional independence and safeguarding AB’s confidentiality?
keypoints: The VHA’s comprehensive electronic health record system contains decades of longitudinal data across the entire system, which can support veterans’ benefits claims.
Veterans must sign an authorization to release health information prior to referral to ensure that information sharing complies with patient confidentiality principles.
The VHA has issued directives requiring VA clinicians to complete non-VA medical and social service forms to facilitate veterans’ benefits applications.
Through the VHA-MLP program, clinicians can submit all medically accurate existing documentation, which attorneys can then translate into legal language to support benefits claims.

title: Make It OK That This Life Is Ending
case:
During Dr. Shen’s shift at the cardiac intensive care unit in his regional hospital, airlift brought in an intubated middle-aged woman in respiratory distress from a rural hospital 150 miles away. Ms. Danforth, previously in good health, had had a massive inferior myocardial infarction earlier that day.
“She was fighting the ventilator so much that we needed to sedate her for transport,” the flight nurse reported. “The patient’s daughter and brother left Orchard Hospital about 15 minutes before we did. They wanted to be with Ms. Danforth at all times.” The ED social worker confirmed at the time of the transfer that the patient’s brother had called from his cell phone to say they were on their way but would have spotty reception for an hour or so as they crossed the mountains.
The patient arrived clammy, with cool and mottled extremities. Monitors showed hypoxia and hypotension. Her heart stopped, at which point, Dr. Shen directed CPR and placed a femoral central line to begin pressors. Over the next half hour, the patient coded several times, requiring stretches of CPR every 5 minutes.
At one point when her heartbeat had resumed, Ms. Danforth’s brother called again. “My sister is a single mom and she’s all that Casey has. Save her at all costs, Doc. You need to keep her alive. We’re only a half hour away.”
The patient slipped back into PEA arrest. After 20 minutes of sustained CPR, Dr. Shen asked if anyone else objected to calling the code—just as the social worker burst into the resuscitation bay to say that the family was 10 minutes away.
case_rewrite:
During Dr. Shen’s overnight shift in the cardiac intensive care unit at his regional hospital, an air ambulance delivered a middle-aged woman intubated and in severe respiratory distress. Ms. Danforth, who had previously enjoyed good health, had suffered a massive inferior myocardial infarction earlier that day at a rural facility 150 miles away.

“She kept fighting the ventilator and we had to sedate her for the flight,” the flight nurse explained upon arrival. “Her daughter and brother left Orchard Hospital about 15 minutes before we did. They insisted on being with Ms. Danforth as much as possible.” The emergency department social worker noted that, as the transfer began, the patient’s brother had called in to say he and the daughter were driving over the mountains and would have unreliable cell service for about an hour.

Upon arrival, Ms. Danforth’s skin was cold and clammy, her extremities mottled. Monitors revealed hypoxia and hypotension. Moments later, her heart stopped. Dr. Shen immediately led the team in CPR and quickly placed a femoral central line to initiate pressor support. Over the next thirty minutes, the patient’s heart arrested several times, each event requiring repeated cycles of CPR, sometimes only minutes apart.

During a brief period when circulation was restored, the phone rang again—it was Ms. Danforth’s brother. “My sister is a single mom—she’s all that Casey has left. Please, doctor, you have to save her. Do whatever it takes. We’re about half an hour away.”

Shortly after, Ms. Danforth went into pulseless electrical activity arrest once more. As the team continued with 20 minutes of uninterrupted CPR, Dr. Shen turned to the group and asked if anyone had objections to calling the code. At that moment, the social worker rushed into the resuscitation bay, breathless, to announce that the family was just ten minutes out.

ethical_issues.question: Should Dr. Shen continue cardiopulmonary resuscitation until the family arrives?
keypoints: After prolonged and repeated cardiopulmonary resuscitation, the likelihood of patient survival with preserved neurological function is exceedingly low; continued resuscitation constitutes medically futile treatment.

As the family’s arrival approaches, the goal of resuscitation should shift from “saving life” to “making the dying process more dignified and acceptable,” reflecting the principles of medical humanism.

Continuing CPR until the family arrives, although of limited medical efficacy, provides an opportunity for the family to say goodbye and prepare psychologically, thereby facilitating their coping with the patient’s death.

Physicians should integrate professional medical judgment, ethical principles, and fiduciary responsibility to balance the patient’s best interests, family needs, and allocation of medical resources in making a comprehensive decision regarding the continuation of resuscitation.

ethical_issues.question: In a resource-limited emergency department setting, should aggressive resuscitative efforts be continued for patients with an extremely poor prognosis, especially when doing so may impact the allocation of resources to other patients?
keypoints: Physicians must prioritize the patient’s best interests under conditions of limited information and urgency, while simultaneously considering the allocation of resources and treatment needs for all patients within the emergency department.

Continuous cardiopulmonary resuscitation consumes substantial medical resources; Dr. Shen must weigh the benefits of ongoing resuscitation against the equitable and rational distribution of limited resources among other patients in the emergency department.

After 30 minutes of intermittent CPR followed by 20 minutes of sustained resuscitation, the patient’s likelihood of survival is exceedingly low, with minimal probability of preserved neurologic function; small ICU case series have demonstrated near-zero survival rates for patients undergoing repeated cycles of CPR, indicating an extremely poor prognosis.

Although continued aggressive treatment may shift toward non-medical goals—such as providing comfort to the family—it nonetheless entails ethical considerations requiring a rational assessment of whether the use of scarce medical resources remains justifiable.

The imminent arrival of the family offers a critical temporal window for decision-making; a brief extension of resuscitative efforts can afford the family an opportunity for closure, facilitating their psychological acceptance of the patient’s death.

Physicians should integrate professional medical judgment, the fiduciary responsibility framework, and principles of medical ethics to comprehensively evaluate the patient’s prognosis, resource availability, and family needs, thereby making difficult yet responsible clinical decisions.

ethical_issues.question: In situations where the patient lacks decision-making capacity, how should physicians balance the patient’s best interests with the emotional needs of the family during the process of futility assessment?
keypoints: Physicians must make timely determinations regarding the futility of resuscitation and act accordingly, centering the patient’s best interests, especially when information is limited and the patient lacks decision-making capacity.
The assessment of futility is a dynamic process that should be continually revised in response to the patient’s evolving clinical status and treatment response.
When family members are imminently arriving, the emergency team may appropriately shift the goals of resuscitation from “saving life” to “making the dying process more dignified and acceptable.”
This shift in goals not only modifies clinical actions but also helps reframe the psychological stance of the healthcare team, facilitating the family’s gradual acceptance of the patient’s death.

From the perspectives of resource allocation and practical benefit, briefly prolonging CPR is reasonable, as it provides the family with a crucial opportunity for farewell and supports their emotional and psychological needs.

Physicians should integrate professional judgment, the framework of fiduciary responsibility, and principles of medical ethics to balance the patient’s best interests with the emotional needs of the family, making difficult yet responsible clinical decisions.

tags: Access to care/Gender or gender transition barriers
title: Should Mental Health Screening and Psychotherapy Be Required Prior to Body Modification for Gender Expression?
case:
Among her patients, Dr. Leonard, a family medicine practitioner, has developed a reputation as a physician especially welcoming to lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, and queer (LGBTQ) patients. Over the years, Dr. Leonard has noticed—with much happiness—that her LGBTQ patients are coming out at younger ages and embracing identities that span an increasingly diverse spectrum of gender and sexuality—an indication, she thinks, that important social and cultural progress has been made.
Today, Dr. Leonard’s nineteen-year-old patient, Tyler, is coming to see her. Tyler was female-sex assigned at birth; however, four years ago, Tyler came out to friends and family as a transgender male. In recent visits, Tyler has begun discussing with Dr. Leonard the possibility of pursuing medical assistance with gender transitioning, including gender-transitioning hormone therapy and gender reassignment surgery, which might involve “top” surgery—breast removal, in this case—or “lower” (also called gender affirmation, gender confirmation, or genital reassignment) surgery, a procedure to change genitalia. On his last visit, Dr. Leonard sent Tyler home with some readings relevant to the next steps of his transition to try to help him decide whether and when—if ever—to pursue gender transitioning with medical interventions.
“Dr. Leonard, I read what you gave me about the next steps of possible treatments, and I wanted to ask you about something,” Tyler said during his appointment. “While I do want to begin transitioning with top surgery, I read that I’ll have to get clearance from a mental health counselor first. But I don’t think my transition should require mental health counseling or approval.”
Dr. Leonard conceded that current guidelines from the World Professional Association for Transgender Health do recommend that patients receive at least an assessment and one documented referral for gender-transitioning surgery from a mental health professional before undergoing a procedure. She suggested to Tyler that he might view mental health counseling as a way to learn about and prepare for the potentially long lasting and irreversible effects of surgical body modification.
Tyler was still resistant. “Dr. Leonard, I don’t think I should have to prove to someone that I am who I am. Who else has to do that?” Tyler added that he had seen counselors in the past—for instance, to help him cope with his parents’ divorce. When it came to gender identity, however, he explained, “It’s the one thing about me I struggle with least. I’m really being pressed to do the counseling to make others comfortable with my decision, so it bothers me that this is pitched as being ‘for my own good.’”
Dr. Leonard had never been prompted to question the process of mental health assessment and counseling for transgender patients in the past. And while her medical judgment still sided with the idea that patients probably benefit from mental health counseling for gender-transitioning HRT or surgery, she also sympathized with Tyler. Dr. Leonard wondered if, in some cases, it could be harmful to ask a patient for identity affirmation through such institutionalized, formal channels. She wondered what to do next.
case_rewrite:
Dr. Leonard, a family medicine physician, is known in her community for fostering a welcoming environment for LGBTQ patients. Over time, she has observed with satisfaction that her LGBTQ patients are coming out at younger ages and are embracing a broad spectrum of gender and sexual identities, which she sees as a testament to positive social change.

One afternoon, Dr. Leonard meets with her nineteen-year-old patient, Tyler. Assigned female at birth, Tyler came out as a transgender male to his friends and family four years prior. During several recent appointments, Tyler has expressed interest in pursuing medical steps for gender transition, specifically hormone therapy and gender-affirming surgeries. He is considering “top” surgery—mastectomy—as well as the possibility of “lower” surgery, procedures intended to align his physical body with his gender identity. At Tyler’s last visit, Dr. Leonard provided him with educational materials about the potential treatments, hoping to help him make an informed decision about whether and when to proceed with medical interventions.

Now, seated in Dr. Leonard’s office, Tyler brings up a concern. “Dr. Leonard, I went through the information you gave me about the next steps, and I have a question,” he begins. “I want to start transitioning with top surgery, but I read that I’ll need to get clearance from a mental health counselor first. I don’t think it’s right that my transition should require mental health counseling or approval.”

Dr. Leonard acknowledges that current standards set by the World Professional Association for Transgender Health recommend at least an assessment and a documented referral from a mental health professional before undergoing gender-affirming surgery. She suggests to Tyler that this process might be seen as an opportunity to prepare for the potentially permanent changes and to more fully understand what the procedures entail.

Tyler remains unconvinced. “I don’t see why I should have to prove who I am to anyone,” he replies. “Who else has to do that?” He mentions that he has seen counselors before, such as when he dealt with his parents’ divorce, but explains, “When it comes to my gender identity, it’s honestly the thing I’m most sure about. It feels like I’m being asked to do this counseling just to make other people comfortable, not for my own benefit. That bothers me, especially when it’s described as being ‘for my own good.’”

Dr. Leonard realizes she has never before truly questioned the process of requiring mental health assessments for transgender patients. While she still believes that counseling can help patients prepare for the lasting effects of hormone therapy or surgery, she empathizes with Tyler’s perspective. She wonders whether, in some cases, the requirement for formal identity affirmation through institutional channels could actually be more harmful than helpful. Faced with Tyler’s concerns, Dr. Leonard finds herself questioning how best to proceed.

ethical_issues.question: Should mandatory mental health screening and psychotherapy be required prior to body modification for gender expression in order to ensure patients’ commitment to and understanding of irreversible surgical interventions?
keypoints: The World Professional Association for Transgender Health (WPATH) recommends mental health assessment prior to body modification for gender expression, in order to help patients understand the irreversibility and potential impacts of surgical interventions.

Psychological assessment and counseling can clarify patients’ motivations and goals for gender transition, identify personal capacities and psychological preparedness that facilitate successful transition, and explore possible physical and psychological effects as well as issues of social adaptation.

Mandatory psychological assessment may be regarded as a form of paternalistic intervention toward transgender individuals, especially given the ethical concern of a double standard, since comparable irreversible body modifications (such as cosmetic surgery) typically do not require similar assessments.

Given that WPATH guidelines allow for flexibility in clinical practice, psychological assessment and counseling should serve as optional supports to facilitate successful gender transition, rather than as mandatory barriers; however, clinicians must exercise caution to ensure the psychological and ethical integrity of patients’ decisions.

Clinicians must ensure that patients provide fully informed consent prior to body modification, understanding the nature of the intervention, anticipated outcomes, risks and benefits, and available alternatives, thereby safeguarding patient autonomy and the quality of decision-making.

ethical_issues.question: Is there a double standard between body modifications for transgender individuals and similar modifications for non-transgender individuals, particularly regarding the mandatory nature of mental health assessment?
keypoints: Non-transgender individuals seeking equally irreversible body modifications (such as cosmetic surgical procedures) are generally not required to undergo mandatory mental health assessments.

Mandating mental health assessments for transgender individuals may constitute paternalistic intervention and warrants scrutiny as to whether it genuinely respects patient autonomy and best interests.
Clinical approval processes for body modification in transgender adult patients involve not only medical risk management but are also influenced by sociocultural norms of gender binarism.

The primary purpose of mental health assessment is to support patients in fully understanding the long-term implications and potential risks of medical interventions, rather than serving as a “gatekeeping” mechanism or proof of identity authenticity.

While ensuring patient safety, it is essential to respect individuals’ self-identity and decision-making authority, and to avoid institutionalized procedures becoming additional barriers or sources of discrimination for transgender persons.

title: How Should Physicians Manage Traumatic Injuries Sustained During Incarceration?
case:
CC is a 43-year-old patient who presented as a high-level trauma code to the emergency department after an altercation in his prison. Epidural hematoma was suspected immediately upon his arrival, and, during diagnostic imaging, CC became obtunded. A neurosurgeon, Dr N, was consulted and CC was taken emergently to the operating room for a craniotomy and transferred to the neurosurgical intensive care unit (ICU). A tracheostomy and percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) tube were placed for prolonged ventilatory support and enteral nutrition.
CC regains capacity to interact meaningfully with others after 2 months in the ICU. CC is weaned from the ventilator, continues to breathe room air via tracheostomy-mask; CC’s central line is removed, but a PEG tube and Foley catheter remain in place. CC is anxious and agitated and does not remember why they are in the hospital. Dr R, a surgical fellow, wonders how to respond to CC’s questions about their injuries, surgical recovery, and returning to prison.
case_rewrite:
CC, a 43-year-old incarcerated individual, arrived at the emergency department as a high-level trauma code following a violent altercation in the prison. Upon arrival, the medical team immediately suspected an epidural hematoma. While undergoing diagnostic imaging, CC became unresponsive. Dr N, the neurosurgeon on call, was consulted and promptly took CC to the operating room for a craniotomy. After surgery, CC was transferred to the neurosurgical intensive care unit (ICU), where a tracheostomy and a percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) tube were placed to provide prolonged ventilatory support and nutrition.

Over the course of two months in the ICU, CC gradually regained the ability to interact meaningfully with others and started to show clear signs of recovering decision-making capacity. CC was successfully weaned from the ventilator and now breathes room air through a tracheostomy mask. The central line was removed, though the PEG tube and Foley catheter remain in place for ongoing medical needs. CC appears anxious and agitated, expressing confusion and repeatedly asking why they are hospitalized. CC does not recall the events leading to the injury or the hospital admission.

Dr R, a surgical fellow, is present during these interactions and considers how best to address CC's questions about the nature of the injuries, the surgical interventions performed, the recovery process, and the eventual return to the correctional facility. Dr R is mindful of the need to respect CC’s autonomy and privacy, ensuring that all necessary information is provided for CC to participate fully in medical decision-making. Dr R is also aware of the importance of maintaining a trauma-informed approach, being transparent about all procedures, and providing CC with opportunities to make choices regarding care. As the time for CC’s discharge and transition back to custody approaches, the surgical team, including Dr R, is working with hospital social workers and coordinating with prison staff to establish a comprehensive medical follow-up and to facilitate access to mental health resources to support CC’s ongoing recovery and reintegration.

ethical_issues.question: How should physicians preserve patient autonomy and privacy in the medical care of incarcerated individuals?
keypoints: Patients who are incarcerated legally retain their right to make medical decisions, and physicians must ensure that the patient or their surrogate receives timely access to the information necessary to exercise medical autonomy.

Capacity assessment should be a dynamic process, wherein physicians continuously evaluate the patient’s cognitive status and preferences through empathic interviewing and open-ended questioning to uphold the patient’s dignity and autonomy.

Implied consent may be applied during emergency treatment; however, subsequent care requires involvement of a substitute decision maker to ensure respect for the patient’s medical decision-making rights.
Correctional staff must not participate in medical decision-making discussions to avoid conflicts of interest and to safeguard the patient’s autonomy.

Physicians should ensure transparency by identifying all healthcare personnel during each encounter, explaining the rationale for treatments performed or recommended, and providing patients with opportunities to make choices.

During sensitive examinations or wound assessments, physicians should inform patients of the expected procedures in advance and provide the maximum privacy possible while maintaining clinician safety, thereby protecting the patient’s right to privacy.

ethical_issues.question: How can trauma-informed care be implemented in emergency surgical management to avoid retraumatization?
keypoints: Clinicians should identify signs of trauma and avoid retraumatizing environments by implementing trauma-informed care through psychiatric screening and respecting patient boundaries.
Ensure transparency and patient autonomy by identifying all healthcare personnel during each encounter, explaining the rationale for treatments, and providing opportunities for patient choice.
For sensitive examinations or wound assessments, clinicians should notify patients of the procedural steps in advance and afford the greatest possible privacy while maintaining safety.
Establish a trusting patient-clinician relationship, with particular emphasis on rebuilding trust in the medical system for patients with prior medical trauma or mistrust.
Initiate mental health services during the emergency phase, including screening for acute and chronic stress disorders, to facilitate psychological recovery.

ethical_issues.question: How should the surgical team ensure the medical transition plan and provision of mental health resources for incarcerated patients returning to custody after surgery?
keypoints: The surgical team must ensure that all active medical problems have ongoing treatment plans and follow-up protocols that can be implemented within the prison setting.

Psychiatric evaluation should be conducted during hospitalization, and trauma-focused therapies should be provided to patients with posttraumatic stress disorder to help develop healthy coping mechanisms.
Hospital social workers and prison social workers need to collaborate in assessing patient needs for both reintegration into custody and subsequent reentry into the community.
Health care practitioners should advocate for incarcerated patients to receive stable interventions and coordinated community care upon reentry.

The surgical team should implement trauma-informed care principles within both hospital and correctional environments and advocate for enhanced mental health resource availability in carceral settings.
